@ncdc

This has come up a bunch in a lot of small ways, and my mind keeps coming back to this idea, so I want to write it down.

In early early kube API we had `desired` and `actual` (or something like that) structs.  Since they were the same struct there were a bunch of things in the struct that were things we didn't want users to set (which eventually became status) and other problems.  As we changed to `spec`+`status`, we lost the concept of "actual" and just folded it into `spec`.

This conflates a couple of ideas and makes it hard to distinguish what the user actually asked for from what was assigned to them.  I am proposing we reinstate that distinction.

I propose that every top-level object (all of them, not just the ones that make sense RIGHT NOW) holds 4 fields (plus ObjectMeta):
- `metadata` - same meaning as today
- `spec` - what the user asked for, with API-defined defaults applied AND NOTHING ELSE.  Every field is available to users.
- `actual` - the exact same structure as `spec`, but with other fields populated  by the system (e.g. nodeName when scheduled, clusterIP when auto-assigned, and so on)
- `status` - same meaning as today

If a user wants to save a pod and move it to another cluster, `spec` is what they want.  If a piece of software wants to know how a pod is operating, `actual` and `status` are what they want.

I chose this layout (which changes the meaning of `spec` because I figured it makes it easier on users to change over, while making it harder for system components (change to `actual`).  This may be wrong, but it's not obvious.  An alternative would be that `spec` means "actual" and `request` means "what the user asked for"; users write `request` and the system writes `spec`.  This has the advantage of software that observes state being easier to change over, but means that users have more pain.  But it is perhaps better to break users, who actually SEE error messages.  Sometimes bigger changes are better because the need to something is obvious (see the bugs people hit with some really subtle v1beta1 -> v1 changes).

It would be nice to make one field writable ONLY by users (create/update) and one field writable only by the system.  We've used binding for scheduling, but there are other things (like clusterIP) that are written directly in-process.  

We could make this round-trippable by embedding `request` under `status` in v1 or even as a new top-level object + sub-resources.

Lastly, there's some question about preserving exactly what the user asked for WITHOUT defaults applied, but I am less sure that is needed or particularly useful.

This has come up a bunch of times in conversations.  The idea is that some external orchestration is being performed and the user needs to signal (SIGHUP usually, but arbitrary) pods.  Sometimes this is related to ConfigMap (#22368) and sometimes not.  This also can be used to "bounce" pods.

We can't currently signal across containers in a pod, so any sort of sidecar is out for now.

We can do it by `docker kill`, but something like `kubectl signal` is clumsy - it's not clearly an API operation (unless we revisit
'operation' constructs for managing imperative async actions).

Another issue is that a pod doesn't really exist - do we signal one container?  Which one?  Maybe all containers?  Or do we nominate a signalee in the pod spec?

Manually-written conversions should be special-cases ONLY.  They should all call the autoConvert... form first.  For example: Convert_batch_JobSpec_To_v1_JobSpec

We have a LOT of manual conversion code, and anything that doesn't call autoConvert first is suspect.

Docker added STOPSIGNAL to let container images change which signal is delivered to kill the container.  Should we support that?  It would be bad if an image expected SIGUSR1 and we sent SIGTERM.

They support ints or strings.  I think we should only support strings.

Seen by inspection, potentially a cause for a user-reported symptom.

Setup:
1. Create a Service, type=LoadBalancer
2. A public IP is assigned
3. Copy that public IP to the Service's locadBalancerIP field

The assumption is that this guarantees your Service will use that IP.  That's true, but it was true without step 3.  If you now delete this Service and create a new one with that loadBalancerIP, it will fail because that IP is not a GCE static address, and was released with your Service.

We should instead throw an error if you do step 3, but have not manually promoted that IP to a static IP through GCE.  This will force users to acknowledge that *they* own that IP, not kubernetes.

This behavior, combined with the fact that we log-and-forget failures to release the IP can result in public addresses being "promoted" to static and left there, without the user having done that action == a leak.

iptables addrtype LOCAL matches anything in the local routing table,
which is broader than we really want.
    
This change expands the hostport filter to check that destination IP is
actually known on an interface.  Unfortunately, iptables can not express
this, so we have to expand the list manually.  To avoid changing 1 rule
to N rules per packet, we pre-filter for LOCAL, and only if that matches
to we run the list of interface addrs.  This requires a new chain.
   
This should be upgrade-safe with no loss of packets.

This is not obviously correct.  It's subtle - a cordon is usually part of a draining operation, so it kind of makes sense to remove things from it.  The service controller removes cordoned nodes from LB pools, but the pods themselves seem to remain in service endpoint lists.  But in the case of an OnlyLocal service, the pods on a cordoned node are cut off.

The GCE L7 LB controller seems to do the same.

For better or worse, we use that Unschedulable flag for a few things. Perhaps we could disentangle it from the "is master" logic (which I think is a taint now?) and change this.

example use case: A customer creates a new node pool and cordons the old one, to move workload.  This would kill traffic to any OnlyLocal Services.

We need a way to know which machines to count as LB targets (gateways)

We need a way to know when a machine is actually going away, so we can remove it from LB pools gracefully.

I don't have bandwidth to drive this right now, but perhaps we need something like graceful deletion or finalizers for nodes?  Or maybe we need a taint that is specific to "do not use for network gatewaying", so reboot operators could be cleaner wrt LBs...  @philips maybe interesting to your reboot operator.

What we SHOULD HAVE DONE, perhaps, is define an empty NetworkPolicyPeer (no podSelector or namespaceSelector or ipBlock) as "none". Perhaps we should do that now, and update docs to say:

Default-deny ingress:

```
kind: NetworkPolicy
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
metadata:
  name: foobar
spec:
  policyTypes: [ "Ingress" ]
  ingress:
  - from: [{}]
```

Default-deny egress:

```
kind: NetworkPolicy
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
metadata:
  name: foobar
spec:
  policyTypes: [ "Egress" ]
  egress:
  - to: [{}]
```

Default-deny everything:

```
kind: NetworkPolicy
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
metadata:
  name: foobar
spec:
  policyTypes: ["Ingress", "Egress" ]
  ingress:
  - from: [{}]
  egress:
  - to: [{}]
```

Why?

1) future rollbacks where a `from` field was newly added will default to fail-closed rather than undefined

2) If we normalize and document this syntax, we have an opportunity in v2 to get rid of the `policyTypes` field and the weird "ingress:[] means deny-all, but ingress:{from:[]} means allow all" semantics.

I do a fair number of code reviews.  I try to pay attention to API validation.  It's gotten very hard to review.

* There are at least 2 util libraries, and the core API validation is effectively a 3rd.

* There are poor conventions across those libraries.

* Commonly used functions might be in any of the 3.

* They are all called "validation".

* Every API version has a "validation" pkg, too.

I'd like to chip away at this and establish better conventions, leading towards a more declarative validation process.  It's not urgent, but I need some code to work on :)

@kubernetes/sig-api-machinery-feature-requests

**What would you like to be added**:

Debugging services often devolves into SSHing to nodes and running iptables or ipset or ipvs commands.  We should have a URL that shows the last written state of these things and maybe a rate-limited way of dynamically reading them.

**Why is this needed**:

Debugging is hard.

Assume I have an object, e.g. a Service, which has a finalizer.  When I delete that object, it correctly waits for the finalizer to be removed.  In that intervening period, I can not see any indication that the object is being deleted.  It does not show up in `kubectl describe`.  It is not an event.  If it gets stuck, I have NO indication that it is being deleted or if I know that, why it is not complete.

**What would you like to be added**:

Some consistent way to see that things are "currently being deleted, waiting for finalizers X, Y, Z"

Unless I am misunderstanding, that Service will select both deployments and will try to route both service ports to both backends.  IOW half the traffic will fail.

There *is* a way to achieve the desired result.  To do this you have to use named ports, rather than numbered (it seems the OP assumed that they behave the same, but they do not).

```
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: frontend
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: exampleapp
      role: fe
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: exampleapp
        role: fe
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: patientplatypus/kubeplayfrontend
        ports:
        - name: frontend
          containerPort: 3000
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: backend
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: exampleapp
      role: be
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: exampleapp
        role: be
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: patientplatypus/kubeplaybackend
        ports:
        - name: backend
          containerPort: 5000
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: entrypt
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  ports:
  - name: backend
    port: 8080
    targetPort: backend
  - name: frontend
    port: 8081
    targetPort: frontend
  selector:
    app: exampleapp
```

Edited to have distinct labels for each deployment.

The strategic merge patch support for service ports is broken (insufficiently unique key) and has been a problem for a long time.  In #101029 we discussed a little whether we could just change it to "atomic".  

It might be simple, but strategic merge patch is too poorly documented to know for sure.  E.g. a quick test:

Given this initial Service:
```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: ports
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: hostnames
  ports:
  - port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    name: tcp
```

...use `kubectl apply` on this:
```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: ports
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: hostnames
  ports:
  - port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    name: tcp
  - port: 80
    protocol: UDP
    name: udp
```

Declarative defaulting is awesome.  For literal values, it is super useful.

HOWEVER...

Many (most) of our default values are `const` and it seems wrong to copy the values into the API comments. For example:

```
type Container struct {
    // ... lots ...
    TerminationMessagePath string `json:"terminationMessagePath,omitempty" protobuf:"bytes,13,opt,name=terminationMessagePath"`
    // ... lots ...
}
```

in defaults.go, we find:

```
func SetDefaults_Container(obj *v1.Container) {
    // ... other stuff ...
    if obj.TerminationMessagePath == "" {
        obj.TerminationMessagePath = v1.TerminationMessagePathDefault
    }
````

If I delete this defaulting code (win!!!) and use declarative defaults, I WANT to say:

```
 // +default=TerminationMessagePathDefault
 TerminationMessagePath string `json:"terminationMessagePath,omitempty" protobuf:"bytes,13,opt,name=terminationMessagePath"`
```

...but that doesn't work.  The defaulter code calls `json.Unmarshall()` and barfs.

I can change that to:

```
 // +default="/dev/termination-log"
 TerminationMessagePath string `json:"terminationMessagePath,omitempty" protobuf:"bytes,13,opt,name=terminationMessagePath"`
```

...but there are at least 2 places of this being needed. That's a risk for drift and bugs.  Other examples like `Protocol` are used ALL OVER.

What I think should happen is that, before calling `json`, we should parse the value.  If it tastes like a go symbol reference, use that.  But it's a LITTLE tricky because of the flat package namespace.  Ideally what we'd get is logic that allows either:

staging/src/k8s.io/api/core/v1/types.go:
```
// +default=TerminationMessagePathDefault
```

...meaning `k8s.io/api/core/v1.TerminationMessagePathDefault` (the package being where the default was used) or else ...

```
// +default=k8s.io/api/core/v1.TerminationMessagePathDefault
```

...being an unambiguous way to use consts in another package.  The default-gen would then ensure that package was imported in the generated code.

And when we render openapi or whatever, we would do the same, but dereference the value so the docs contained the literal.

Thanks to @liggitt for spotting this one ex post facto.

Prior to 1.22 a user could change NodePort values within a service during an update, and the apiserver would allocate values for any that were not specified.

Consider a YAML like:

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: foo
spec:
  type: NodePort
  ports:
  - name: p
    port: 80
  - name: q
    port: 81
  selector:
    app: foo
```

When this is created, nodeport values will be allocated for each port.  Something like:

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: foo
spec:
  clusterIP: 10.0.149.11
  type: NodePort
  ports:
  - name: p
    nodePort: 30872
    port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 9376
  - name: q
    nodePort: 31310
    port: 81
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 81
  selector:
    app: foo
```

If the user PUTs (kubectl replace) the original YAML, we would see that `.nodePort = 0`, and allocate new ports.  This was ugly at best.

In 1.22 we fixed this (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/103532) to not allocate new values if we still had the old values, but instead re-assign them.  Net new ports would still be seen as `.nodePort = 0` and so new allocations would be made.

This broke a corner case as follows:

Prior to 1.22, the user could PUT this YAML:

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: foo
spec:
  type: NodePort
  ports:
  - name: p
    nodePort: 31310 # note this is the `q` value
    port: 80
  - name: q
    # note this nodePort is not specified
    port: 81
  selector:
    app: foo
```

The `p` port would take the `q` port's value.  The `q` port would be seen as `.nodePort = 0` and a new value allocated.  In 1.22 this results in an error (duplicate value in `p` and `q`).

This is VERY minor but it is an API regression, which we try to avoid, and the fix is not too horrible.

This commit adds more robust testing of this logic.


/kind bug
/kind api-change
/kind regression

```release-note
Fix a small regression in 1.22 in Service updates. The circumstances are so unlikely that probably nobody would ever hit it.
```

xref https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/103532/files#r694831725

Cherry pick of #104601 on release-1.22.

#104601: Fix a small regression in Service updates

This has now manifested as user-reported outages (errant controller looping and allocating ports causing DoS in HA apiservers), hence the backport.

It is not uncommon for users to Create a Service and not specify things
like ClusterIP and NodePort, which we then allocate for them.  They same
that YAML somewhere and later use it again in an Update, but then it
fails.

That's because we detected them trying to set a ClusterIP from a value
to "", which is not allowed.  If it was just NodePort, they would
actually succeed and reallocate a new port.

After this change, we try to "patch" updates where the user did not
specify those values from the old object.


/kind bug
/kind api-change

```release-note
Fix a small regression in 1.22 in Service updates. When using `kubectl replace` (or the equivalent API call) on a Service, the caller no longer needs to do a read-modify-write cycle to fetch the allocated values for `.spec.clusterIP` and `.spec.ports[].nodePort`.  Instead the API server will automatically carry these forward from the original object when the new object does not specify them.  
```

Change the build_binaries path to use modules

This makes "new" and "old" setup_env functions. In subsequent commits,
all callers of the "old" form will be fixed, and the "new" will be
renamed back.

The old and new functions diff:

```diff
--- /tmp/a	2023-12-14 09:02:57.804092696 -0800
+++ /tmp/b	2023-12-14 09:03:09.679999585 -0800
@@ -1,4 +1,4 @@
-kube::golang::old::setup_env() {
+kube::golang::new::setup_env() {
   kube::golang::verify_go_version

   # Set up GOPATH.  We have tools which depend on being in a GOPATH (see
@@ -7,9 +7,9 @@
   # Even in module mode, we need to set GOPATH for `go build` and `go install`
   # to work.  We build various tools (usually via `go install`) from a lot of
   # scripts.
-  #   * We can't set GOBIN because that does not work on cross-compiles.
-  #   * We could use `go build -o <something>`, but it's subtle when it comes
-  #     to cross-compiles and whether the <something> is a file or a directory,
+  #   * We can't just set GOBIN because that does not work on cross-compiles.
+  #   * We could always use `go build -o <something>`, but it's subtle wrt
+  #     cross-compiles and whether the <something> is a file or a directory,
   #     and EVERY caller has to get it *just* right.
   #   * We could leave GOPATH alone and let `go install` write binaries
   #     wherever the user's GOPATH says (or doesn't say).
@@ -20,16 +20,6 @@
   #
   # Eventually, when we no longer rely on run-in-gopath.sh we may be able to
   # simplify this some.
-  local go_pkg_dir="${KUBE_GOPATH}/src/${KUBE_GO_PACKAGE}"
-  local go_pkg_basedir
-  go_pkg_basedir=$(dirname "${go_pkg_dir}")
-
-  mkdir -p "${go_pkg_basedir}"
-
-  # TODO: This symlink should be relative.
-  if [[ ! -e "${go_pkg_dir}" || "$(readlink "${go_pkg_dir}")" != "${KUBE_ROOT}" ]]; then
-    ln -snf "${KUBE_ROOT}" "${go_pkg_dir}"
-  fi
   export GOPATH="${KUBE_GOPATH}"

   # If these are not set, set them now.  This ensures that any subsequent
@@ -40,24 +30,10 @@
   # Make sure our own Go binaries are in PATH.
   export PATH="${KUBE_GOPATH}/bin:${PATH}"

-  # Change directories so that we are within the GOPATH.  Some tools get really
-  # upset if this is not true.  We use a whole fake GOPATH here to collect the
-  # resultant binaries.
-  local subdir
-  subdir=$(kube::realpath . | sed "s|${KUBE_ROOT}||")
-  cd "${KUBE_GOPATH}/src/${KUBE_GO_PACKAGE}/${subdir}" || return 1
-
-  # Set GOROOT so binaries that parse code can work properly.
-  GOROOT=$(go env GOROOT)
-  export GOROOT
-
   # Unset GOBIN in case it already exists in the current session.
   # Cross-compiles will not work with it set.
   unset GOBIN

-  # This seems to matter to some tools
-  export GO15VENDOREXPERIMENT=1
-
-  # Disable workspaces
-  export GOWORK=off
+  # Explicitly turn on modules.
+  export GO111MODULE=on
 }
```

Looking at validation code, I see places that are using the last argument to `field.Required` that do not need to be.  It's extra detail that is almost never needed.  For example:

```
field.Required(fldPath.Child("type"), "must specify a metric source type"))
```

This seems totally redundant - the error already says that a field named "type" is required.  It is occasionally useful to add additioninfo, such as:

```
field.Required(fldPath.Child("maxUnavailable"), "cannot be 0 when maxSurge is 0"))
```

Likewise, `field.Invalid` has details which are more often used, but the details should not repeat the field name.

Also, sometimes Invalid is used, when required would be better:

```
allErrs = append(allErrs, field.Invalid(fldPath, "", "bootstrapToken or file must be set"))
```

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/111697 documents not to do this.  Would be good to make it visible when/if it does happen.

Create a single-port Service of type LB.  It gets nodeport 31234.

Wait for an LB IP to be assigned.

Access that LB IP on 31234 - it works as long as firewalls don't stop it.

This is because the NodPorts chain doesn't necessarily check IPs (hence why it is the last chain).  It finds port 31234 and forwards it to the Service.

I think we need to add rules to "KUBE-PROXY-FIREWALL" to allow valid IP+port combos and then drop other uses of those IPs.  Or something.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/113245#discussion_r1009911070

Validation allows:

```
        ports:
        - containerPort: 93
          hostPort: 9376
        - containerPort: 93
          hostPort: 7693
```

but server-side apply does not.

As of 1.29 we can SSA unrelated fields in an object with "dup ports" and even do SSA "repairs" to those fields.  What we can't do is SSA them with this state in the apply.  Important: this is  allowed (more by accident than design) and semantically unambiguous.

The defined SSA key is not sufficiently unique (`ontainerPort + protocol` -- needs to include `hostPort`). To evolve the key is tricky, but the only real alternative is atomic, which has it's own issues.

Is it worth trying to find a safe way to expand an SSA key?

It looks like label keys "controller-uid" and "job-name" are used.  Unprefixed label keys are reserved for end-users - these should be "something.kubernetes.io/...".

Since this is in the wild, the best we can do would be to define and document the proper names, set and accept both for a while, then stop setting the old name, but still accept it forever.  Something like that.

Given this deployment:

```
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: multiport
spec:
  replicas: 0
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: multiport
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: multiport
    spec:
      containers:
      - image: k8s.gcr.io/serve_hostname:v1.4
        name: c1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 93
          protocol: TCP
        - containerPort: 93
          protocol: UDP
```

```
$ k expose deploy multiport
The Service "multiport" is invalid: spec.ports[1]: Duplicate value: core.ServicePort{Name:"", Protocol:"UDP", AppProtocol:(*string)(nil), Port:93, TargetPort:intstr.IntOrString{Type:0, IntVal:0, StrVal:""}, NodePort:0}
```

Looking at staging/src/k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/cmd/expose/expose.go, it builds a `protocolsMap` which reduces those 2 ports to one protocol.  It should probably just return a list of {proto, port} or even {proto, port, name} which it extracts from the target.

Fix prerelease codegen log string

Systemd works because it knows the connection was established to this
machine and this port. Handing off a file descriptor is easy in that
context. The 5-tuple is unchanged.

K8s doesn't know which node to run the new pod on until the traffic has
arrived. You can't be guaranteed to start a pod on that specific node. If
you start a pod elsewhere, the routing layer needs to know about it.

So you need a different mechanism. For example, the sending-side needs to
pause the traffic upon TX, wait for a pod, then route to it.  Now you have
to get that signal out from the node(s) to the upscaler.

Not impossible but not as easy has passing an FD.

comment_text:
Yeah, this is kind of a mess because of a lot of reasons.  Until workspaces lands, we are forced to phony-up a GOPATH.  Go's default is to put the module cache under GOPATH[0], which means it gets stored in this directory tree.

Running in a work tree and then running one of these scripts means the GOPATH changes and the modcache is found to be empty, so it re-downloads.

This change explicitly points the caches to the "real" tree, so we avoid the re-download, but as you point out, it is not very well managed.

I wish I could say workspaces fixes this, but it doesn't.  That tree (for now) STILL sets up a phony GOPATH, though for a different reason - `go install`.  We can't just set GOBIN (not compatible with crossbuilds).  We *could* use `go build -o` but then EVERYONE needs to get it just right (and a trailing slash matters, if I recall).  So the only way I found to make it easy to build tools was to set GOPATH and use `go install`, which writes to GOPATH[0]/bin.  PUUUUUUUKE.  I'd much rather run with GOPATH=/dev/null.  Better ideas are welcome :)
code_context:
@@ -521,7 +521,10 @@ kube::golang::setup_env() {
   kube::golang::create_gopath_tree
   export GOPATH="${KUBE_GOPATH}"
 
-  export GOCACHE="${KUBE_GOPATH}/cache"
+  # If these are not set, set them now.  THis ensures that any subsequent
+  # scripts we call (which may call this function again) use the same values.
+  export GOCACHE="${GOCACHE:-"${KUBE_GOPATH}/cache/build"}"
+  export GOMODCACHE="${GOMODCACHE:-"${KUBE_GOPATH}/cache/mod"}"

Documenting some experiments:

A standard patch cycle looks like:

```
$ # We have 1 port defined
$ curl -s -k -XGET \
    -H "User-Agent: curl" \
    -H "Accept: application/json" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
    "https://$IP/api/v1/namespaces/default/services/hostnames" \
    | jq .spec.ports
[
  {
    "name": "p1",
    "protocol": "TCP",
    "port": 80,
    "targetPort": 9376
  }
]

$ # this is what a client-side "kubectl apply" patch looks like
$ # note that this patch is valid - ports have different numbers
$ cat p1.json
{
  "spec":{
    "$setElementOrder/ports": [
      {"port":80},
      {"port":81}
    ],
    "ports":[
      {"name":"p1","port":80,"protocol":"TCP","targetPort":9376},
      {"name":"p2","port":81,"protocol":"UDP","targetPort":9377}
    ]
  }
}

$ # The patch operation works - 2 ports
$ curl -s -k -XPATCH \
    -H "Content-Type: application/strategic-merge-patch+json" \
    -H "User-Agent: curl" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
    -H "Accept: application/json" \
    "https://$IP/api/v1/namespaces/default/services/hostnames?fieldManager=kubectl-client-side-apply&fieldValidation=Strict" \
    -d "$(cat p1.json)" \
    | jq .spec.ports
[
  {
    "name": "p1",
    "protocol": "TCP",
    "port": 80,
    "targetPort": 9376
  },
  {
    "name": "p2",
    "protocol": "UDP",
    "port": 81,
    "targetPort": 9377
  }
]
```

No matter what we do, this pattern must still work.

A patch which uses the same port number and different protocol looks like:

```
$ # client-side apply only uses port number as the key, so it generates this patch:
$ cat p2.json
{
  "spec":{
    "$setElementOrder/ports": [
      {"port":80},
      {"port":80}
    ],
    "ports":[
      {"name":"p1","port":80,"protocol":"TCP","targetPort":9376},
      {"name":"p2","port":80,"protocol":"UDP","targetPort":9376}
    ]
  }
}

$ # The result is corruption - only one port (but a different protocol!)
$ curl -s -k -XPATCH \
    -H "Content-Type: application/strategic-merge-patch+json" \
    -H "User-Agent: curl" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
    -H "Accept: application/json" \
    "https://$IP/api/v1/namespaces/default/services/hostnames?fieldManager=kubectl-client-side-apply&fieldValidation=Strict" \
    -d "$(cat p2.json)" \
    | jq .spec.ports
[
  {
    "name": "p2",
    "protocol": "UDP",
    "port": 80,
    "targetPort": 9376
  }
]
```

That is clearly broken, even though the client tried to do the right thing.

As far as I can tell, there is no way to specify a compound `mergeKey`, and
snooping thru code I see that carried as a string and used as a key to index
into maps which model the type.  IOW "port+protocol" would not work as a key.

So let's do some experiments.

1) Remove the `patchStrategy` and `mergeKey` from the `ports`. field

Regen openapi, rebuild apiserver, deploy.

```
$ curl -s -k -XPATCH  \
    -H "Content-Type: application/strategic-merge-patch+json" \
    -H "User-Agent: curl" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
    -H "Accept: application/json"
    "https://$IP/api/v1/namespaces/default/services/hostnames?fieldManager=kubectl-client-side-apply&fieldValidation=Strict" \
    -d "$(cat p1.json)"
{
  "kind": "Status",
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "metadata": {},
  "status": "Failure",
  "message": "The order in patch list:\n[map[name:p1 port:80 protocol:TCP targetPort:9376] map[name:p2 port:81 protocol:UDP targetPort:9377]]\n doesn't match $setElementOrder list:\n[map[port:80] map[port:81]]\n",
  "code": 500
}
```

Hmm, that's not good.  Let's try the second case anyway.

```
$ curl -s -k -XPATCH \
    -H "Content-Type: application/strategic-merge-patch+json" \
    -H "User-Agent: curl" -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
    -H "Accept: application/json" \
    "https://$IP/api/v1/namespaces/default/services/hostnames?fieldManager=kubectl-client-side-apply&fieldValidation=Strict" \
    -d "$(cat p2.json)"
{
  "kind": "Status",
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "metadata": {},
  "status": "Failure",
  "message": "The order in patch list:\n[map[name:p1 port:80 protocol:TCP targetPort:9376] map[name:p2 port:80 protocol:UDP targetPort:9376]]\n doesn't match $setElementOrder list:\n[map[port:80] map[port:80]]\n",
  "code": 500
}
```

So ... fail.

Let's try `patchStrategy=replace` (which I can't tell if it is supported or not, but is used at least one other place).

That yields the same errors as above.

Let's try the `retainKeys` strategy, which I don't fully grok, but ... YOLO?

Same.  Sigh.  So none of those options seem compatible.

What if we YOLO harder and change the key completely?  How about "name".

```
  "kind": "Status",
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "metadata": {},
  "status": "Failure",
  "message": "map: map[port:80] does not contain declared merge key: name",
  "code": 500
}
```

OK, that's reasonable.

So I still do not see a path to fixing this and working with older clients unless we want to make meaningful changes to strategic-merge-patch code to allow compound keys AND to allow partial specification of those for legacy clients.

Here's an option, maybe.

What if we added a new tag to this field, like `avoidMerge`, which can be used by enlightened clients to say "oh, I shouldn't use this".  What they do with that information is situational.

e.g. `kubectl edit` could see that and fall back on `PUT` instead of `PATCH`.

e.g. `kubectl apply` could either fall back on PUT or just issue a warning that "this might not do what you want".

Since it is on the `ports` field, any operation that doesn't touch that field would be unchanged.

Existing clients who do not know this new tag just keep doing what they were doing.  If it worked for them, awesome, if not, well...it's not any worse.

I dug into the code a bit.  It looks like `apply` calls `strategicpatch.CreateThreeWayMergePatch()` and `edit` calls `strategicpatch.CreateTwoWayMergePatch()`.  If those functions took an optional "respectMergeAvoidance` flag, the callers could fall back on more primitive methods.

hack/make-rules/build.sh sets all sorts of things that may or not matter for these "build tools".  I can go wither way.  As part of go workspaces, I was assuming build.sh was a better destination, since it ensures that the build output is local (_output/bin, local cache, etc.).  If any tool-script forgets to call `golang::setup_env` it will splat things into the users own GOPATH, which is a) obnoxious and b) hard to debug when there are `client-gen` (etc) binaries in your own GOPATH and the local _output.

With go workspaces, vendoring is not supported, so we need to figure out how we want to do that - one option is to commit a full module cache, which requires that we set GOMODCACHE.

I agree that `go install` is simpler and more obvious.  `GO111MODULE=on` will not be needed on workspaces lands.  `GOPROXY=off` is not compatible with workspaces.  So we will end up with "naked" `go install` which is nice - except for the MODCACHE stuff, which maybe we can fix in different ways?

I can go either way - drop this PR or keep?

I know first-hand that changing the per-node mask in-place is NOT a good idea.

Nobody had an obviously better answer for you.  I think your choices are to try expanding to the next-adjacent space (possibly restarting all apiservers and maybe other things) or to bring up a new cluster with a different config (e.g. a different space with a smaller per-node mask) and migrate to it (which could also solve your "need to upgrade" problems).  Neither answer is awesome.

In newer releases we have an actual API to allow multiple pod CIDRs.

I'm going to close this issue because it is pretty clearly not a "bug" in Kubernetes, but we can still discuss your options.

deepcopy happens in the main deepcopy codegen - it does a find acrss the whole codebase, which is possible because it always generates into the same project as the input.  clients generate into a different project, which makes them uglier.

scripts we call (which may call setup_env again) use the same values.

simpler.

comment_text:
> Terminating API server pods (running as static pods) should report a readiness of false

This is not true in general.  Terminating pods should continue to be probed for readiness (or we need to change downstream consumers of this API which assume that they are).

> Readiness going to false allows for the loadbalancers and endpoints to gracefully remove the pods without throwing 500s.

Endpoint management generally removes all terminating endpoints, whether they are ready or not.  The difference is only important when we have no viable ready, non-terminating endpoints - then we can fall back on ready-but-terminating endpoints.

This is an interesting idea, but I wonder if it's a sub-case of a more general capability.  Something like "tolerance" (bad name because it's too close to tolerations)  - which allows a pod to express bounded-duration situations which it can or cannot deal with.

"I can accept up to 30 seconds of node-unready; after that, kill this pod"

"I can accept up to 5 minutes of pending; after that, kill this pod"
comment here?

/approve

Thanks for the repro.  For regular pods I riffed on it a bit to prove to myself that it is how I think it should be:

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: "readiness-term-pod"
  namespace: "default"
spec:
  terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60
  restartPolicy: Never
  containers:
  - name: test
    image: "k8s.gcr.io/busybox"
    command:
      - sh
      - -c
      - "_term() { echo TERM; } ; trap _term SIGTERM; while true; do rm /tmp/ready; sleep 10; touch /tmp/ready; sleep 10; done"
    readinessProbe:
      exec:
        command:
        - cat
        - /tmp/ready
      failureThreshold: 1
      initialDelaySeconds: 2
      periodSeconds: 2
```

Then:

```
$ while true; do k get pod readiness-term-pod -o json | jq '.status.conditions' | grep -B1 '"Ready"'; sleep 2; done
    "status": "True",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "True",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "True",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "True",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "False",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "False",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "False",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "False",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "False",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "True",
    "type": "Ready"
    "status": "True",
    "type": "Ready"
```

Then delete the pod and see it still flips from read to unready and back.

So we believe this is good and safe on  GCP, Azure, and AWS.

The scary thing about how cluster autoscaler works today is that is has quietly assumed this would be a non-issue all along.  If there's any cloud in which IS NOT safe, cluster autoscaler works by winning a race condition.

This PR just makes that race window 0.  CA will never win :)

So it would be super if a) someone from AWS side and Azure side can confirm; and b) people from other providers could weigh in.

Thanks for following up!

comment_text: Rebased

Calling update-codegen.sh with no arguments runs all the functions in definition order.  Client-generation depends on protobuf, so protobuf codegen needs to be near the beginning.

Also add some debug output for protobuf generation.

Also hides some old, verbose debug output.

/kind cleanup
/sig architecture

```release-note
NONE
```

Swagger "docs" are actually Go code, which is used by other codegen tools, so if you really want to regen EVERYTHING, this is part of it and sequence matters.

This includes #115245 just because of the diff context.

/kind cleanup
/sig architecture

```release-note
NONE
```

/kind cleanup
/sig architecture

```release-note
NONE
```

/kind cleanup
/sig architecture

```release-note
NONE
```

Changing the default is not something we can do.

Imagine that you are a user, perhaps a bank or large retailer or even just a small software-services shop. You have scripts and tools which turn up clusters and oversee upgrades. Your apps all use ".cluster. local" names to reference each other. 

Then we come along and say "Oh, we made a mistake. It maybe doesn't affect you but we're changing it to .cluster. lan.". All your scripts are broken.  New clusters don't work.  Your apps can't find each other.

How much time do you think this change is worth to the average customer?  1 hour?  1 day?  1 week?  I think it would cost more like months of engineering time at the larger customers (and many of them have my email address :)

It's just not feasible or justifiable unless we have either a MAJOR problem it causes or else a notable version jump (e.g. kubernetes v2).  The only other option might be some DNS schema reboot which was totally opt-in.

I'm sorry. I love cleaning up old messes, but the price of this is just not the same order of magnitude as the value.

If someone wants to think about a DNS schema reboot, let's talk.  But it's no small task, I think.
/retest

> What I think should be considered is changing the default for the next major release.

Sure, we'll consider it for kubernetes v2.

Edit: Sorry, that was tongue-in-cheek.  There are no plans for a v2 at this time.

I would support an effort to update docs and to note that "while cluster.local is the default, we strongly recommend users to use .lan or some other name that doesn't have the same issues that .local does"  (and link to the wikipedia or something).  Heck, we can even print big red warnings if you use .local (in tooling that we control).

Ideally this should return a functional object which can pass validation and be used in code.  If you apply this cleanup to other test-cases you will start to find all those fields.  Not blocking this PR for it, just direction.

An "environmental suitability check" :)

I don't think you want to define it exclusively (or maybe at all?) in terms of conditions, but I haven't thought about that.

The one that came up in the past was "node unready" and/or "node unreachable".  Some apps have no state and want to be killed more "eagerly" if the node seems down.  Other apps have local state that may be expensive to rebuild, and would prefer to wait longer.

I could see "disk full" being another or "node OOM rate".
@msau42 wrt stateful stuff
I'm not sure that keeping this open as an issue is useful any more?

>  It can be dealt with the same process as API deprecation, can't it ?

The process of deprecating APIs has been SO painful for people that we have stopped enabling pre-GA APIs by default.  The math works on paper, but real life just plays out differently.  Changing this default roughly means: get *everyone* in the *whole world* who has tooling around cluster turnup to pay attention, and to take non-trivial action to update those tools, and to validate every application they deploy into k8s, in order to solve a problem most of them don't have.

> it seems to me that the impact would be way lower than an api deprecation, which requires users to adapt their yaml files

You're not counting all the work that needs to happen to qualify these new clusters in legacy environments, and you are ignoring all the apps that run in those clusters.  If any one of them assumes a .cluster.local name, or has a config that specs a .cluster.local name, they will break.  And you and I can not predict anything about the nature of that breakage or the impact.

You're also ignoring the complexity of making people live in a mixed mode for an unknown amount of time.  Users can not upgrade all their clusters at once, so their pipelines and config and tooling and apps all need to know WHICH suffix is in use in a given cluster.

All this cost to solve a problem that doesn't actually hurt most of them.  If this was actually a problem for most people, they would have been screaming about it these last 8+ years. 

It's just not going to happen.   I truly regret not making a better choice so long ago, and I am sorry to the folks who ARE impacted, but I don't see how to justify pulling the rug out from the rest of the k8s userbase.

We can make the issue more visible.  We can fix docs and even issue warnings.  We can blog about it and tweet about it and even get up on the KubeCon keynote stage and rant about it.

But we can't _impose_ that change in a breaking way.

@alandsidel 

> causes a little pain for people who have to adapt

Put yourself in the shoes of a company who has dozens or hundreds of clusters, with thousands of internal users of those clusters and hundreds of apps.  Some of those apps are in-house built, some are helm charts you downloaded, and some are commercial.  You regularly turn clusters up and kill off old ones.

Kubernetes devs say "We're changing the default DNS suffix".

How do you react (assuming you actually see the change coming up, which is not 100%)?  You have three choices:

1) Adopt the change.  Re-qualify your clusters and EVERY APP in them.  Educate your users.  Update your internal docs.  Change your apps to handle both styles of clusters, and re-deploy them all.  Make sure everything you depend on is updated - service mesh, helm charts, controllers, DNS, LB integrations, etc.  Roll it out one cluster at a time, with people in your NOC ready to abort when something goes wrong.  Estimate: 100+ aggregate SWE-years and 3+ calendar years.

2) Reject the change, change your tooling to specify `.cluster.local` rather than rely on the default.  Re-qualify your clusters.  Roll it out one cluster at a time, with people in your NOC ready to abort when something goes wrong.  Estimate: 2+ aggregate SWE-years and 1+ calendar years.

3) Stop upgrading.  Estimate: 0 SWE years up front, until it all explodes and you get a critical compromise and have no good upgrade path.

Again, this is not actually a problem for most people, or they would have fixed the issue, as you did.

> the ongoing API changes are much more disruptive than this change would be

a) I disagree.  API changes are EASY by comparison.
b) The amount of pain API deprecations have cause has forced us to change tracks.  We have squandered TOO MUCH goodwill on this topic.

> I encourage @thockin and others to reevaluate their positions.

I _have_ re-evaluated my position.  I once thought that changes like this were reasonable.  I do not any more.  And don't get me wrong, I am SAD about it, but that doesn't change my position.  I am sad about a great many things that I can't fix.

Is there a canonical example of what a best-practice, no-baggage, max-leverage, no-BS component's `main.go` should look like?  If I were writing a NEW component, what code would I copy-and-extend?

There are so many public functions here now, I can't keep track of who calls what, and what I am supposed to do - what is the north star (is that hemispherist?) ?

I'm sorry if I came off as defensive.  I don't feel defensive, at least not of my own past mistakes.  I do feel the need to defend our global user-base's time, though :)  I have come to really internalize the notion that they have very limited and finite time to spend on "running in place" and I want to make sure that when we ask them to do that, it is justified.

> A ludicrous worst case scenario isn't a good supporting argument

I talk to a LOT of Kubernetes users.  You don't get to handwave away the reality of the rest of the industry.  I'm glad to hear that you have figured out "the right way" - please get the literal MILLIONS of other clusters to do it that way, then report back.

> API changes routinely disrupt ops

Which is why Kubernetes has gone to great lengths to stop hurting our users.  We call this "learning from our mistakes".

> It's clear I and others here won't change your mind. Maybe in the future someone else will inherit your position and make a different decision.

If there's someone who wants my position, please drop me an email or something.  I'd be glad for some more help.

> At personal capacity, I still think the SCTP support need make sense at k8s level.

I don't think anyone here disagrees, but sig-net lacks _active_ representation of this user-base.  Without a champion or champions, SCTP support will forever be a niche thing that isn't well supported.  I don't just need bug reports, but easy repros, test cases, and ideally hands-on-keyboards from people who know and live it.

I know TCP and UDP pretty well, and that covers 98% of today's k8s customers.  If we want to expand to new use-cases, we need some experts to chime in.  (and my thanks to those people who have!)

> I don't quite understand why this would be a lot of work for folks

And therein lies the difference of opinions :)

> but why would they need to re-qualify their clusters

Asking large enterprisese to change ANYTHING is a lot of work, and asking them to change something SO FAR DOWN the stack, even if we pinky-promise it is just codifying the existing behavior, will force them to re-qualify their workflows and apps.

Is it *really* a no-op?

Did they find *all* the places they need to set this?  If they miss even one it could be catastrophic.

What other work are they NOT going to do in order to make time for this?

How do they explain to their VP that one of their k8s experts (highly sought, hard to hire, probably well paid) is going to spend the next few weeks (best case) or more (more realistic) spinning up new clusters, running tests, and trawling through the code base to ensure that nothing changes at some future date.

The risk (to our users) of imposing this default change is not proportional to the reward.  Ask the dockershim-removal team how eager they are to do that again, but bigger.

> Clearly many of us believe this one is.

We have different definitions of "many".

> > You don't get to handwave away the reality of the rest of the industry.
> 
> I'm not.  If they rolled out a test cluster and encountered all the problems inherent in having internally adopted and used the .local domain, they have a real alternative option to the three cherry picked ones you provided:  Try again and manually select cluster.local as their domain, the same way you use the option now to use something else.  Bang.  Everything works again.  No "100+ SWE years", no change in tooling, and no deciding to not get updates.  Just a small change to the deployment.

You're REALLY under-stating this.  ANY CHANGE is expensive.  This particular change has SO MUCH risk.  A single test cluster does not begin to capture it.  Literally EVERY SINGLE APPLICATION has to be evaluated.  One errant hardcoded hostname in an app or a config and you could take down your business.

And please don't forget that we have customers with LITERALLY *thousands* or clusters.  We have clusters running on airplanes, and in research physics, and in governments, and in banks, and stock exchanges, and in baseball parks, and ...

You're asking them ALL (or almost) to take action and assume this risk.  For almost no gain.

For those people who can't use .local, they've already fixed it locally.

For those who can, they don't want your default change.

This literally helps NONE of our existing users.  So spend your passion on FUTURE users and make really good warnings when they do the "dangerous" thing.

> > Which is why Kubernetes has gone to great lengths to stop hurting our users. We call this "learning from our mistakes".
> 
> Maybe defensive wasn't the right word.  Patronizing perhaps.  In any case, there's only one step left after learning from your mistakes:  correcting them!

We are correcting them - we don't ask users to change things that already work in order to not get broken by future Kubernetes versions.

> Meanwhile, tucked away somewhere in the internal documentation somewhere is a note that says to be careful you do supply a  DNS domain even if it's made up, because if you don't, .local is going to get used and break half the devops team's access.  Eventually this note will be lost and forgotten and we'll repeat the whole painful process again.  Meanwhile every day new admins all over the world are taking their first steps into the world and running headlong into the issue for the first time.

So go make the tools spit out a warning.  Docs are necessary but not sufficient.

> > If there's someone who wants my position, please drop me an email or something. I'd be glad for some more help.
> 
> I'm sure they're out there, tongue in cheek as this reply may have been.

I'm really not kidding.  Step 1 is "show up and do the work".

Go nuts!  I am not shying away from feedback, but this project is not a direct democracy :)  FWIW, I *have* discussed this with a few other project leaders and not one I have spoken to (which is not all of them, so ACK the selection bias) thinks this would be a good idea.

We should be careful about how we describe what this is for.  Regardless of how we document it, people will find other ways to use it and we should be sure it can't impact the whole cluster.

For example, we could say that this is intended for pods which are particularly sensitive to particular external conditions, and can help rectify certain classes of transient bugs or situations which could be resolved by choosing a different node.  It is not intended to be a user-defined affinity system.

We need to avoid situations like a "query of death".  E.g. consider that an attacker figures out how to make a pod crash a node.  They submit this pod and set one of these tolerances, so that it will very quickly get moved to another node, then another, then another.  They could kill a whole cluster.

Some of them *could* be node issues.

@@ -94,3 +98,48 @@ function kube::protoc::diff() {
     exit 1
   fi
 }
+
+function kube::protoc::install() {
+  (
+    local os
+    local arch
+
+    os=$(kube::util::host_os)
+    arch=$(kube::util::host_arch)
+    download_folder="protoc-v${PROTOC_VERSION}-${os}-${arch}"
+    download_file="${download_folder}.zip"
+
+    cd "${KUBE_ROOT}/third_party" || return 1
+    if [[ $(readlink protoc) == "${download_folder}" ]]; then
+      kube::log::info "protoc v${PROTOC_VERSION} already installed. To use:"
+      kube::log::info "export PATH=\"$(pwd)/protoc:\${PATH}\""
+      return  #already installed
+    fi
+
+    if [[ ${os} == "darwin" ]]; then
+      # TODO: switch to universal binary when updating to 3.20+
+      url="https://github.com/protocolbuffers/protobuf/releases/download/v${PROTOC_VERSION}/protoc-${PROTOC_VERSION}-osx-x86_64.zip"
+    elif [[ ${os} == "linux" && ${arch} == "amd64" ]]; then
+      url="https://github.com/protocolbuffers/protobuf/releases/download/v${PROTOC_VERSION}/protoc-${PROTOC_VERSION}-linux-x86_64.zip"
+    elif [[ ${os} == "linux" && ${arch} == "arm64" ]]; then
+      url="https://github.com/protocolbuffers/protobuf/releases/download/v${PROTOC_VERSION}/protoc-${PROTOC_VERSION}-linux-aarch_64.zip"
+    else
+      kube::log::info "This install script does not support ${os}/${arch}"
+      exit 1
+    fi
+    kube::util::download_file "${url}" "${download_file}"
+    unzip -o "${download_file}" -d "${download_folder}"
+    ln -fns "${download_folder}" protoc
+    mv protoc/bin/protoc protoc/protoc
+    chmod -R +rX protoc/protoc
+    rm -fr protoc/include
+    rm "${download_file}"
+    kube::log::info "protoc v${PROTOC_VERSION} installed. To use:"
+    kube::log::info "export PATH=\"$(pwd)/protoc:\${PATH}\""
+  )
+}
+
+# Marker function to indicate protoc.sh has been fully sourced
+kube::protoc::loaded() {
+  return 0
+}

@bowei - keep or close?
With cloud-providers moving out of tree, this really becomes a provider thing, not a k8s thing.

Ingress has `ImplementationSpecific` path-types, which is the only reasonable way to do this.

Ingress also supports wildcards for hostname matching.

Beyond that, Ingress as an API is effectively "done".  Further work needs to aim at the Gateway API https://gateway-api.sigs.k8s.io/

There are actually a few issues intermingled here.  I've heard at least the following requests from people:

1) Treat network bandwidth like CPUs - a specific request and limit, guarantee a min and enforce a max.  Example: Given a node with a 10Gb NIC, my pod is guaranteed 1.5 Gb, but can burst to 5Gb.

2) Provide priority-bands for access to bandwidth without specific numbers being specified.  Example: This pod is p0, so  should be able to use the NIC whenever it needs, in preference to p1 pods.

3) Provide a way to express a minimum bandwidth need.  Example: This pod needs to run on a machine with at least 4Gb network - less than that is not acceptable.

4) Provide a real-time signal of congestion and use that to relocate pods.  Example: If my pod can't get 4Gb/s at p90 over 2 minutes, kill it and pick a different node.

I think all of these are interesting in their own ways, but they all have downsides, too.  Number 2 is actually under discussion, kind of, in this KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/3004

Before we implement the other ideas, we'd need to do some deep thinking about the tradeoffs.

I'm open to these discussions and even KEPs, but we need to anchor it in REAL users.

I'm going to re-title this issue to be more discoverable.

For future readers:

Port ranges specifically is problematic because implementations like IPVS do not support them well and because NodePorts are very limited.

Doing whole-IP forwarding (either L4 or L3) is plausible but the work on that stalled (https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/2611) for lack of  someone to drive it.

It may be better to focus on Gateway API (https://gateway-api.sigs.k8s.io/) as the vehicle to enable this, long term, but it doesn't seem impossible to do it in Service, if we can justify the use.

From sig-net discussion: This is mostly cosmetic - assigning a ClusterIP is pointless because they aren't useful in these cases.  It's not that they are actively harmful.

This is sort of what Gateway is designed for, even if it doesn't have all the pieces you want yet.  Ideally, Service's role is primarily to define a grouping with a few common properties, and Gateway defines all the "entry points" (what Ingress was originally intended for) into that grouping.

Service's "concentric" model is very deeply rooted and I don't think we want to add opt-outs for every entrypoint (cluster IP, node port, externalIP, LB) - even though we do it for NodePort.

If we explore Gateway and find it*can't* work for this, I'd reconsider.

So - shall we keep or close this?

> If there is not already, there must be some way in the Gateway API of allocating virtual IPs

Gateway as an API does not handle this - it's the domain of Gateway controllers which implement specific classes.  E.g. the Google Cloud controllers can use GCP's IP-Address APIs.

So for you, if you need an external IP allocated, your controller needs to know where it comes from.

This idea was more like:

* Node says "I have 110 IPs".
* Some component adds 1 "ip" resource to each pod (except host network)
* Scheduler knows whether it can or cannot assign a new pod to a node by the "normal" resource counting.

Multi-network makes this harder, but also easier in that (some of) this info is being surfaced.

xref https://github.com/kubernetes/community/pull/7063

I am sure that such collisions are rare (though I didn't see a metric - could add one!) but it seems surprising that our API offers a "generate a name for me" option which can fail with "name in use".

The docs used to imply that apiserver will retry upon name-conflict, but it does not seem to actually do so.

See staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go `Store.Create()` which calls `objectMeta.SetName(e.CreateStrategy.GenerateName(...))` one time, before passing off to `e.Storage.Create()` and then looking at the result with `rest.CheckGeneratedNameError()`.

I thought maybe this is retried higher up-stack, but I couldn't find it and @lavalamp and @liggitt seemed to concur.

We agreed it was surprising and this issue is a chance to discuss if it is a bug or not.
"resource" here is like "cpu" and "memory" not like "pods" and "services" :)

saying "don't do it".  I think it's worth exploring to see if it is as bad
as we all suspect.  The client-go issue is a significant one, though, and I
am not sure you can overcome it, as much as that PAINS me.

The random-suffix length comes out of the total available name length,
which is somewhat restricted for some resources.  Making it longer just
reduces the user-available size.  We already do not handle this very
cleanly (e.g. deployment can be long, then replicaset adds a suffix, then
pod adds a suffix).

This is all true, and yet, in practice, it hasn't been a major problem.
Adding a single extra random digit grows from ~14M possible to ~387M - but
I am not sure it's worthwhile.  If Daniel's math is right (I didn't check
it, but I have no reason to doubt) it is a pretty small risk.  Adding to
that, the largest user of generateName is ReplicaSet, which will retry on
failure anyway.

So maybe the answer here is just "add a metric".

It's also worth noting that the random suffix added to each replicaset is
ELEVEN characters (10 + "-").  That seems excessive.  If we made that be 6
(or seven) and made generateName be 6 (or seven) we'd have shorter names
overall, lower risk of leaf-pod collisions, and almost certainly still be
random enough.  Also it seems Deployment->RS doesn't use generateName - not
sure why, maybe it's a hash of something (they all look like hex), which
might make sense - but I didn't look.  Still it could

We could go one further and change `generateName` to take a configurable
suffix length, so if there are extra characters available we can use them
e.g.:

```
# uses a standard 5 character suffix
generateName: foobar-
```

```
# uses a 7 character suffix
generateName: foobar-*******  # could be XXXXXX like mktemp,but * seems
less ambiguous
```

That's pretty compatible.  Or even simpler, make generateName use at least
5 characters, but up to 8 if the space is available.  Or even randomize the
suffix length between 5 and 8 - giving > 293 *billion* possible names.

All this without adding a retry loop.  Worthwhile?

comment_text:
There are four ways to set default values.

1) Make it a pointer, set it from your controller.  This is not really a "default" because clients can observe the field as unspecified (nil).

2) Make it a pointer, set it from defaults.go.  Clients should never see the field as unspecified.  This is necessary when the Go "zero value" is in-bounds for the field (to distinguish zero-value from unspecified)

3) Don't make it a pointer, set it from defaults.go.  Clients should never see the field as unspecified.  This only works of the Go "zero-value" is out of bounds for the field.

4) Don't make it a pointer, let the Go zero-value be the default.

I do not see explicit default-setting code for this field, so it is either case (1) or case (4).  It is a pointer, so it must be case (1).  IIUC you are proposing to make it case (4).  I write this to call out that (2) is also available to you.

Considerations:
* Changing the field to a non-pointer is "simpler", especially if you never defined what it means to be unspecified.
* Changing the field to a non-pointer is a breaking change for client-go users, but not k8s API users.
* The fact that we have `StartedAt` and `Started` kind of makes me grumpy, but no fixing it now.

We usually argue for (2) because of consistency ("all optional fields are pointers, period") but it is not UNIVERSALLY upheld by reviewers.  But your case is even more interesting, because this field is inside `ContainerStatus`, which itself is a list, and so it defaults to `[]` (empty).

So even if you did (2), you don't have any `ContainerStaus` objects to initialize.  But the same is true with (4)!!

TL;DR: `Pod.Status.ContainerStatuses[]` is an empty list until someone (kubelet) populates it.  Given that someone has to explicitly populate that list, when would this default ever kick in?  Are you trying to support a new field with old clients who don't know they are supposed to set it?

I think you can still do (2) or (4), just not a CREATE time.  Given that it would be a breaking change to client-go, I'd prefer (2) if you really need to do this.  See pkg/apis/core/v1/defaults.go

As a project maintainer, I really want us to do the type split, and I really don't want "you can't use this here" fields.

To do the type split properly we either hugely impact client-go or we write a NEW client-lib that doesn't expose all of our internal gunk, and actually has API guarantees.  I think we should do that, but it's REALLY not a side-quest.

That said, I am not willing to block the development of sidecars KEP on this HUGE, unstaffed, and _guaranteed_ contentious effort.

So with great regret, I think the best course of action here is to add the field to Container and ensure that it doesn't get used outside of InitContainers, with a link back to this issue explaining why we are self-flagellating.

If someone wants to undertake the type-sharing problem, I am happy to offer my time to help figure out how.

This old issue was revived by the bot.

We discussed today at sig-net whether / how k8s proper could take on this problem.

The consensus points I took away were:

1) We should probably indicate where such policy rules would go in the Gateway API.
2) We probably don't need to own all the expressions of such policy - L7 could mean HTTP (which can get arbitrarily complicated) but it could also mean MySQL or Kafka or Redis.  I don't think k8s should own all of those.  Gateway already owns *some* policy-ish definitions for HTTP, and it seems reasonable that *very common* ones find their way into that set.

This seems like a place where the extensible core + ecosystem implementations will be a better solution.

Given that, we're closing this issue.

> generally it seems like FQDN is being used to introduce CNAME records into Kubernetes's DNS services

This seems more like a bug than an intentional feature.  We have a supported way of introducing a CNAME record - `Service.spec.type: ExternalName`.  We should not add another equivalent-but-different mechanism.

>> With ingresses dropping support for ExternalName we were planning on knative/serving#11821 to using EndpointSlices with FQDN. The reasoning being is users don't interact with EndpointSlices and operators can use RBAC rules to limit their creation/modification - but allow the Knative control plane to do so.
>
> This does feel marginally safer, but I'm still not sure how safe it would be. 

My thoughts exactly.  Epsilon safer than a literal CVE is not "safe".

> Knative creates K8s services of type external name which point to a cluster local proxy (typically the proxies service dns name - istio-ingressgateway.istio-system.svc.cluster.local). This allows us to perform L7 type routing within a cluster without a mesh.

And this works, right?  As long as you don't try to create an Ingress.

Let's recall what that CVE was about - it was about not letting user B expose user A's workloads to user C (canonical example of C being "the internet"). 

Thinking out loud: 

@robscott this kind of feels like a job for ReferenceGrant, doesn't it?

If one were to drop a refgrant into namespace `istio-system`, expressing "allow access to Service istio-ingressgateway from namespace *", it seems clearer that the controller should allow it.  But actually, wait.  That doesn't distinguish between internal and external use, and once you have access to the proxy, I guess it uses hostname routing?

So could I do this:

User A has a service X intended only for internal traffic, ExternalName = the cluster proxy.
User B creates a service Y, ExternalName = the cluster proxy
User B gives service Y an internet-facing Ingress, which gets IP 1.2.3.4
User C accesses 1.2.3.4 with the hostname header set to "x.a.svc.cluster.local" 

User B has done exactly what that CVE tried to prevent.

Changing ExternalName to FQDN EPSlice limits this only to users who can write EPSlices, which is a small consolation.  Fundamentally you have a shared proxy, whose routing instructions are in the attacker's hands (hostname).  That just can't work, unless the routing is taken out of the attacker's control (e.g. destination IP or port).

Please forgive me if I am making some incorrect leap in logic.
Smells like a bug to me.

> You're example doesn't really apply because Knative has a common use case where people create internal Knative Services and then expose a few them to the internet using a DomainMapping feature. But we have complimentary resources for the operator to limit who can create what mappings.

Once traffic arrives at the proxy, how does the proxy decide which backend service to route to?

/lgtm

Sweet dreams!

Copying here for simplicty:

Problem 1:  `Validate` calls `generateSelector`  which adds labels.  This is wrong - `Validate` is not supposed to modify objects!  This should be done from `PrepareFor*` hooks instead.  A side-effect of this is that any update operation will set these labels, which seems like a benefit but is hidden in a place I would NEVER think to look.

I would retool all of this.  `PrepareForCreate` should call `generateSelectorIfNeeded`, which checks the `manual` flag and sets the labels.  It's not clear to me whether that should happen from `PrepareForUpdate` or not.  It's easy, but a bit unusual (to set unrelated fields) and low-value (I think).

Problem 2: `Validate` says `if job.Spec.ManualSelector == nil || *job.Spec.ManualSelector == false` - this is wrong.  That field should be recognized as `nil` in pkg/apis/batch/v1/defaults.go and assigned a value.  You should not be nil-checking fields all over the place and inferring their default values.  It seems innocuous on a bool, but imagine an int or string field - you'd have the default value sprinkled all over the codebase.

Also, this test is not super useful without some checking of WHY the error happened.  It should do a count check (e.g. "I got 1 error, but expected 2") and it should do a substring check ("the error should say 'Invalid: metadata.name' somewhere"). When I, personally, work on an effort of this magnitude, I know that my ability to guess which parts go into which "stage" is low.  I would do those "prefactoring" things you *know* you need, but move quickly into the process of building the feature (second stage), which will inevitably uncover more things you can do to prefactor, or even just peel off independently useful changes which can be reviewed and merged in isolation.  Eventually, you will have that "API change" PR and you'll realize that not much more can be stripped out of it. 

But you won't know when you're done until you start.

So yes, do the obvious "before" parts, but also - start the "during" work ASAP.  Focus on an end-to-end working example - don't make it clean.  Don't write good tests.  Hack.  Add commits for each small step.  Make it work, and keep a "TODO" list of everything you find along the way.  Once you have a demo-able "Look!  It works!" branch, THEN go back and peel things out, send PRs, and rebase the "big branch".  The commits have to accumulate before they can decrease.

> Gateway TCPRoute/UDPRoute usually reference Services, but it can be any other objects, we can use EndpointSlices but we need something to generated them, Rob suggested using Deployments/statefulset that makes more sense to me (complexity will go to the controller though)

I had a discussion a while back (but have not had a chance to actually write a KEP or anything) about decomposing Service further.  The core of a Service is a selector.  I see an increasing number of things that want approximately a selector, but without all the other Service stuff.  Why couldn't we have that as an API?

The ugly problem I haven't worked through is ports and port mapping.  EndpointSlice isn't just a list of IPs, it's also the result of service->pod port-mapping (including named ports).  I was thinking maybe the API looks like:

```
apiGroup: core/v1
kind: PodSelector
metadata:
  name: my-selector
  namespace: my-app
selector:
  matchLabels:
    foo: bar
ports:
  - name: http
    portNumber: 8000
  - name: https
    portNumber: 8443
  - name: optional
    portName: admin
```

This generates EPSlices, but instead of a label `kubernetes.io/service-name` it gets `kubernetes.io/pod-selector-name`.

I think it's a trap - someone will eventually add an `echo` after it and spend hourse debugging why the echo doesn't fire.

I mean, not ME...I would never do that (again), but someone might.

comment_text:
The Service API is too overloaded and sort of land-locked by past decisions.  `PodSelector` is the minimized, scrubbed version of headless.

Could we find ways to bend Service into that shape?  Maybe, but it would be slow and painful while we change validation rules across several releases.  Also goes to the "whole IP" services topic.

Help Wanted

On Wed, Mar 1, 2023 at 2:20 PM Tim Bannister ***@***.***>
wrote:

> The Service API is too overloaded and sort of land-locked by past
> decisions. PodSelector is the minimized, scrubbed version of headless.
>
> Look forward to the KEP!
>
> —
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/56440#issuecomment-1450931897>,

> .
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.Message ID:
> ***@***.***>
>

> The only thing really that knows how expensive a pod is to delete is the pod itself

I think there are cases where that is true, and cases where is is false.

Lots of discussion on https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/3190

e.g. https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/3190#pullrequestreview-868561920 was my initial reaction, much of which still seems appropriate to ask.
suffxed?
YOu removed the template - on purpose?
Where is this coming from?  Why is it in this PR?
You could just rename `etpLocalNodePredicates` -> `stableNodeSetPredicates` ?

I thought we landed on *not* using the taint - since all the major cloud providers can delete a VM  from the LB automatically, there's no reason to do it ourselves, right?

In  https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/pull/3460#discussion_r1093923670 I mentioned excluding a tainted node if it was not already in the LB set, but we don't have neough information here to do that, I guess.

comment_text: s/Fo/For/ ?
/approve

I don't think the answer is to stick it into KubeletConfig, which is not easily (or at all) accessible in some environments.

If we wanted to do this, we're sort of talking about an API resource, a la `GatewayClass` or `StorageClass`.  I'm not endorsing this idea yet, just saying that it's the logical conclusion.  Ultimately, everything that can be configured fits this pattern 

The truth is that there's already a workaround (admission control).  It's not an awesome workaround, but it exists.  That, by definition, makes it lower urgency.  We have a LOT of irons in the fire right now, and I don't think we have capacity for it today.

I'll accept it, and we can track it over time, but even if a volunteer showed uptoday, I don't know that we can absorb it right now.

/triage accept
any updates here?

Discussed at sig-net:

The original use-case cited here (an LB over VKs) seems dubious, but I buy that there could be real use cases, and we agree this hidden restriction is pretty horrible.

The fact that we have this ONE port special-cased an not kube-proxy or other things is ugly. 

I don't know how we can assert that it's safe without auditing ~all providers.  We could add a flag (puke) like `--allow-service-port-10250` so that providers can set it.  I don't love it.
Should we call this a dup of #56440 ?

Maybe we can big-hammer this?  If the file changes, `os.Exit()` - it's clumsy but it should work?

I don't know that we want arbitrary "where clauses", but as long as this is hypothetical - maybe?  Simpler is better, so I'd really need to see concrete use-cases to justify

comment_text: I'm OK either way.  I see your point.

> I thought we landed on not syncing load balancers as a consequence of the addition/deletion of this taint, but making sure we never add nodes to the LB set which might have this taint

Agree, but is this what is happening?  The informer setup code filters node updates, but if a node is added or removed, we sync all LBs, and in doing so we get all nodes and then filter by stableNodeSetPredicates, which includes the taint.

So chanign the taint won't trigger a sync, but any node being added or removed would cause all taineted nodes to be removed?  I don't think that's what we want, is it?

comment_text:
Thanks!

/lgtm
/approve
Add helper funcs to clean up svc controller tests
Remove obviously redundant tests

/kind cleanup

```release-note
NONE
```

Another example:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/commit/3d63aed2dca23bfec17c174b32b63c0be511fe85

after that, it was obvious we have redundant test cases:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/commit/b5e4bbb132d0d6c4079e5c98a3bb8ab6e88be381

Similar to #114511

Table-driven tests are awesome, but they can be very verbose and obscure what's actually being tested in each case.

I started on cleaning up staging/src/k8s.io/cloud-provider/controllers/service/controller_test.go, but I only did two tests - there are more.  This is a good opportunity for someone to read some code, so some cleanup, and score a PR or two towards org membership or similar :)

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/116259

I did: 

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/commit/3d63aed2dca23bfec17c174b32b63c0be511fe85

after that, it was obvious we have redundant test cases:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/commit/b5e4bbb132d0d6c4079e5c98a3bb8ab6e88be381

> What's the over/under on how long until this gets reverted?

> in alpha clusters with all alpha feature gates enabled, kubelet is panicking

I would not have guessed 3 days :)

Two things:

1) the way to do this is to pass the validator to `ValidateObjectMeta` above - it currently passes `apivalidation.ValidateReplicationControllerName` which is an alias to `apimachineryvalidation.NameIsDNSSubdomain`.  You probably want `apimachineryvalidation.NameIsDNSLabel` (or if I am right on 2, a custom function that is similar)

2) IIUC, this is a breaking change - Jobs which used to pass validation will no longer pass, and if any such Job is saved in the API, it might not be updateable.  The only way this is acceptable is if you can show that *all* such Jobs could not possibly have worked, under any circumstances.

Is that true?  I thought the "manualSelector" feature meant that a user COULD create a long-name Job and use their own selector and it would be OK?

So at best, you have to check the "manualSelector" field here, I think.

But also, labels can contain dots, so a Job named "foo.bar" curtrently passes the name validation and label validation, but would fail to pass DNS-label validation.  Breaking change.


Here's how I would sequence the issues I flagged:

1) Fix the registry logic so that validate does not mutate the object.  Move the selector generation to PrepareForCreate() and fix up the tests.  This sets the stage for...

2) Add test cases for long names, both with and without manual selectors.  I expect this will fail after the above, but with an unintuitive error.  Fix validation so the name validation is correct.

3) Fix up the default value setting for `job.Spec.ManualSelector == nil` -> `false`.  Find the places that check for nil and simplify.

Interesting - I wrote a similar benchmark to convince myself that change was OK and I found the delta to be unremarkable vs. the code clarity.

Can you post the test?

Thanks!

/lgtm
/approve
