I don't think we should roll back until we're sure. 

> For Istio only the external listener is exposed via a Service type=LoadBalancer. Same for Contour.

listener here meaning a distinct TCP port with distinct routing rules attached which are only evaluated if the client's original destination was provably an external LB?

How do we PROVE that?

Assume internal-only service A, type "ExternalName", which aliases to your proxy.  Your proxy listens on :443.

In-cluster client accesses the "cluster-local" name (which might not actually be ".cluster.local", but I assume you handle that).  That name is CNAME to your proxy on :443.  Proxy accepts the connection and looks at the hostname header.  It finds "A...local" and routes back to service A.

Repeat for any number of services, B, C, D, ...

Someone wants to put their service X on the internet.  They create an LB which routes hostname "external.example.com" to an IP, which is delivered to your proxy on :8443 (different than above).   Proxy accepts the connection and looks at the hostname header.  It finds "external.example.com" and routes back to service X.

Presumably the route-map for :8443 is different than for :443.  If a connection arrives at :8443 with hostname header "A...local", it has to be an error, right?

But what ensures that the LB config used :8443 and not :443?  Who is configuring that, and via what API?

I'm not against anyone spending time thinking about it, but I want to be clear that the priority of this is probably lower than most of the other in-flight KEPs, so it's possible it won't get the largest share of attention (IOW - slower feedback)

Even if I wanted to bring that API back, I couldn't do it until 1.28.  I'd prefer to implement the equivalent behavior in the newer framework, which leaves more room for implementations, and get THAT into 1.28.

I appreciate the patience here, @dprotaso .  I'm trying to see the whole picture :)

So, as a user, I build my knative app and deploy it.  Why do I need a Service?  Is it *just* to get an internal name?

I want my internal name to send traffic to some other managed proxy (the mechanism is what we are discussing) in another namespace.

The managed portion of the stack has/will/can establish LB(s) which bring traffic into the cluster and to the managed proxy.  The proxy is clever enough to not route to knative services which didn't ask for it.

Right so far?

ExternalName services *do* work (though clunky for internal use, IMO).  An in-cluster user who access such a service will find a CNAME to the real proxy (which presumably has the original service name in its TLS certs?).

But why does the external LB->proxy path need the ExternalName, if it already knows the Service?  If traffic already arrives at the proxy, isn't that enough?

I can buy the stutter in this case.   I do find `restartPolicy: Container` or `restartPolicy: Pod` to be awkward, so `restartPolicy: (NotRequired | RestartContainer)` is not so terribly offensive to mine eyes, with "RestartPod" or "Forbidden" being plausible extensions. 

I'd still go with "NotRequired" instead of "RestartNotRequired" - the stutter doesn't add any clarity there.

comment_text:
RS doesn't use `generateName`:

pkg/controller/deployment/sync.go
```
    // Create new ReplicaSet
    newRS := apps.ReplicaSet{
        ObjectMeta: metav1.ObjectMeta{
            // Make the name deterministic, to ensure idempotence
            Name:            d.Name + "-" + podTemplateSpecHash,                                                                                                                     
            Namespace:       d.Namespace,
```

This is, obviously, broken.  Deployment name is validated with `NameIsDNSSubdomain`.  So is ReplicaSet.
So if Deployment.name is too long, ReplicSet creation just fails.  https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/116447

But let's assume that was fixed.  :) 

No matter what deployment name we feed it, we get a replicaset with a 10 character suffix (well, 10 + '-'), potentially at the cost of the last few characters of the deployment name.

Then, no matter what replicaset name we feed into `generateName`, we get a pod with at least 5 characters of suffix, again at the expense of the last few characters of input.

So what pods would fail?  We're only using extra space *if* we can do so and stay less than 63.  We don't truncate any extra.  Worst case is exactly the same as today, unless I am missing something?

I'm not convinced we need to be so clever.  This is intended not to break (at least not to fail validation) anything that works today - at the extreme edge it becomes the same as today - 5.

What it *might* break is anything that assumes the suffix is always 5 characters, but those things are arguably already broken, they just don't know it.

Also, I think it is useful that names of the same prefix get suffixes of the same length.  We could randomize the max from 5-10, but then UIs won't line up :)

Shouldn't this describe what it is for, the valid options, and the limitations on its use?

I know we talked about using different values vs same values - does it need to be the same Go type, or should we define a new Go type with comments that reflect the per-container use?

There are some (like Service will eventually create an IP resource, but they are 1:1, not 1:N).  I can't think off the top of my head of anything that "thingy makes collection-of-thingy".  And if we did they are all either self-truncating or subject to the same "too long" GIGO problem.
I take that back - Service -> EndpointSlice is an example, but service names are short (DNS Label) and EPS names are long (DNS Subdomain).

But that's a problem today, this just makes it 5 chars sooner.

E.g. A -> B -> C.

len(A.name) is 63
len(B.generateName) = 63 -> B.name = A[0:58]+rand(5)
len(C.generateName) = 63 -> C.name = B[0:58]+rand(5) = A[0:58]+rand(5)

It's unfortunate that C lost its name-link to B, but it should still be reasonably-likely unique.

Unless a C is always derived from a B, it doesn't know to do middle-out truncation.  In hindsight maybe we should have tracked the original name + generated portion for this use-case, but...not all problems are worth fixing?

> What if generate name detected names that contain a generate-name produced suffix 

Why?  The only property we need to (approximately) ensure is uniquess.  I doubt it's worth setting up something so easy to trip over - what's the ROI ?
Why do we need this in Ephemeral?  It's not actually usable here, right?

I know you're following the established pattern here, but I think this inside-out style negatively impacts the readability.  As an example, from below:

```
addServicePort(newService("udp-service", "", v1.ServiceTypeLoadBalancer), v1.ProtocolUDP),
```

vs
```
newService("udp-service", "", v1.ServiceTypeLoadBalancer, addPort(v1.ProtocolUDP)),
```

I would suggest something like:

First commit which changes this "inside-out" into the "tweaks" model, but doesn't change any coverage.
Second commit adds some tweak functions and changes callers to use them.

I would also suggest breaking this up into a series of smaller commits, each of which does one cleanup.  This is much easier to review.

We can do that - I can just straight change 5 to 10, nothing will break.  Mostnames will just get 5 charts longer.  Long names will get a little less prefix and a little more suffix.

I figured we're getting away with 5, so why churn if not needed, but that's such an edge case.  It is simpler just to go big.  We could do 8 instead of 10, too.  Still pretty unique :)

Sure.  SGTM.

You're just racking up the PRs ;)

Feel free to send it with your other one included so I can pipeline the approvals.
Don't yhou want to keep both of these?  Or is alpha1 just nuked totally?

Don't think of it as "a backport".  You're extending the v1alpha1 API.

What I am looking for in a change like this is: what happens if a v1alpha1 user sets BOTH fields?  It looks like you are asserting that `Context` is a superset of `ResourceHandle`, and `Context[0]` must match it.  At least, that's what the "internal" -> v1alpha1 seems to do.

We will have to tackle that later.

We have these scripts in `staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator` called "generate-groups.sh" and "generate-internal-groups.sh".

External projects which want kube-style code generation (deepcopy, conversion, openapi, client, etc) call them.  We emulate that pattern for in-tree things like `k8s.io/kube-aggregator`, `k8s.io/sample-apiserver`, `k8s.io/metrics` and more (see `hack/update-codegen.sh` for full list).

If you manually remove all the generated files from, for example, `staging/src/k8s.io/kube-aggregator`, and then re-run its own `update-codegen.sh`, you might expect all the files to come back.  You would be wrong.

Some (prerelease-lifecycle) have no support in "generate-groups".

Openapi, in particular, is weird. There is a generated openapi file in `.../kube-aggregator/pkg/generated/openapi/zz_generated.openapi.go`, but `.../kube-aggregator/hack/update-codegen.sh` doesn't ask for openapi generation.  It is covered by the (very twisty) openapi logic in `k/k/hack/update-codegen.sh`.

If you add openapi to the `.../kube-aggregator/hck/update-codegen`, it generates an equivalent file, but in a different place!  The result is `.../kube-aggregator/pkg/client/openapi` rather than `.../kube-aggregator/pkg/generated/openapi`.

Why?  Because invoking `generate-internal-groups.sh` passes an "output dir" of `.../pkg/client`, so obviously output goes there.

A simple fix would just be to consume openapi from `.../pkg/client`, but it's not obvious that openapi should be in a "client" directory.

A more involved fix would be to break down and replace or eliminate these "generate-groups" scripts, which are proving somewhat brittle and hard to maintain.  We could leave them for external clients but offer better alternatives, perhaps.

The benefit of making the subproject scripts handle openapi is that the (again, very twisty) openapi logic in `k/k/hack/update-codegen.sh` could be removed.  That would be nice.

I don't have time to wrestle with this right now, so I am leaving it "broken" and filing this bug, hoping maybe someone with better context will chime in.

comment_text:
> Does that put implementation-specific topology modes at a disadvantage relative to official ones?

I think that, if there was a need and it was reasonable, we could consider additional hints.  Unfortunately, our API and our implementation are entangled and we don't have an awesome way to extend existing resources.

In a perfect world, hints would be an extension of the base type, but there's just no way to express that.  We could put all hints in a parallel type, but now we have wonky races where the slice is updated but the hints are not, and we 2x the API ops.  We could render hints as a per-slice annotation.  That's probably the "fairest" approach, but ick.

I expect that other implementations won't use EPSlice anyway, so it's moot

> why is this an annotation but the EndpointSlice Hints are a field

This is fair - `topologyMode` could be a field.  As much churn as this has undergone, that would require resetting to alpha.  I think I'd just as soon run with annotation and if this pattern sticks, we can eat the tech-debt of converting to a field.

Agree or disagree?

Copying context from other PR:

https://go.dev/play/p/L9rXnbLzuOu

Spew is unmaintained and large. I am sure we could fork it and make it do what we want but....ugh

kr.Pretty is already used transitively, though I can't see how to make it's output not include newlines.

github.com/kylelemons/godebug/pretty is good output but seems abandoned


litter is nice output, but is a net new dep. If we could get 3 or 4 deps which use spew to switch, we could justify this as a one-for-one.

Plan: 
1) move this kubernetes/kubernetes/pkg/util
2) convert all callsites in k/k to use this new function (same PR)
    == We should have only one place that uses spew
3) we work with a few deps to kill off spew
4) we convert it to litter or something else
    == No deps on spew
5) Move to kubernetes/utils

condition (Foo vs example.com/foo)?

comment_text:
I am skeptical that the hash logic ever did was intended - without `DisablePointerAddresses` set, you can't hash two equivalent objects unless they have NO pointers in them.

So I want to say that we should change this to include `DisablePointerAddresses` and `DisableCapacities`, but I don't know what that would break.  There are not too many call-sites, so I think we should go through them all and verify either that I am wrong and it works, or I am right and changing it would not destroy things.

In particular the kubelet checkpoint is suspect to me.

comment_text: Why are these in this test?  I don't think we need them, much less in this unrelated test.
typo: warnings

/approve

just small nits left

> Indeed, at the moment we're simply checking if the --resolv-conf option is set or not. I was thinking of checking if the set value is an existing file or not, but that would have set the expectation that the contents of the file would have been taken into account, which we're not. Although, that might be an interesting option to have - have a value like HostResolvConfig which will take into account the default host configuration, or set the value to a resolv.conf file, reading and applying its content. WDYT?

I don't know enough about Windows to know whether pointing it to a UNIX-standard (not Windows) file makes sense, or if there's an equivalent Windows-standard file.

Backing up:

If this flag is not set, it gets defaulted to "/etc/resolv.conf" (pkg/kubelet/apis/config/v1beta1/defaults.go).  That does not appear to be flag-gated, and flows down into the DNS configurer (pkg/kubelet/kubelet.go), so (unless I am missing something) it will ALWAYS have a value unless explicitly set to "".  Which it is in cmd/kubeadm/app/componentconfigs/kubelet_windows.go, so I guess that's close enough.

On Linux, this flag always means a file-path.

Is that what you want it to mean on Windows?  I am reading "no".  If that's true, then:

a) Don't rename the field as I suggested (sorry!)

b) Define which exact magic string windows user must use here, and fail anything else.  If you think you MIGHT want to point to a file later, then define the string as something that can't be a filename?  There aren't really any such strings on Linux, so you may have to eat into the same ID space.  E.g. "Host" means "read the host config" not "read a file named "Host"?

This is fine as a continuation of what we've always done, but the REAL fix would be to load conntrack info and scan that for tuples that don't match and need to be discarded.  It works as is, but if anything goes wrong, we lose the "event" and never come back to reconcile.

> if there's not a kubelet approver with context who can say whether this should apply to static pods or not, are we confident leaving this in for 1.27?

That's the question, innit?    @derekwaynecarr @mrunalp @SergeyKanzhelev @bobbypage - this whole feature is at risk of rollback.  Help?

I think we could get away with saying that static pods are explicitly not supported for now, but I'm not sure that solves the problem.  Are we OK with a gate which can disrupt more than its own feature?  It seems fair game to me, but not ideal, obv.
The question is - who will take ownership for this PR review or make the call to back it out and try again next time?

> plain TCP vs RAW
> -- I see the confusion, how about k8s.io/raw-tcp ? (I am also ok with only k8s.io/raw but wanted to be more explicit)

I'm advocating for just "kubernetes.io/raw" - it's effectively a sub-protocol, and "TCP" has already been denoted in `protocol`.  I could also leave it out and debate the naming after this goes in.
We can fix things like gRPC over TLS after code freeze - the main goal is to get this in.  Make the cosmetic changes and ping.
What's the rationale for not having this be the default behavior, like it is on Linux?

+
+package util
+
+import (
+	"fmt"
+	"net"
+	"strings"
+
+	"k8s.io/klog/v2"
+	netutils "k8s.io/utils/net"
+)
+
+const (
+	cloudProviderNone     = ""
+	cloudProviderExternal = "external"
+)
+
+// parseNodeIP implements ParseNodeIPArgument and ParseNodeIPAnnotation
+func parseNodeIP(nodeIP string, allowDual, sloppy bool) ([]net.IP, error) {
+	var nodeIPs []net.IP
+	if nodeIP != "" || !sloppy {
+		for _, ip := range strings.Split(nodeIP, ",") {
+			if sloppy {
+				ip = strings.TrimSpace(ip)
+			}
+			parsedNodeIP := netutils.ParseIPSloppy(ip)
+			if parsedNodeIP == nil {
+				if sloppy {
+					klog.InfoS("Could not parse node IP. Ignoring", "IP", ip)
+				} else {
+					return nil, fmt.Errorf("could not parse %q", ip)
+				}
+			} else {
+				nodeIPs = append(nodeIPs, parsedNodeIP)
+			}
+		}
+	}
+
+	if len(nodeIPs) > 2 || (len(nodeIPs) == 2 && netutils.IsIPv6(nodeIPs[0]) == netutils.IsIPv6(nodeIPs[1])) {
+		return nil, fmt.Errorf("must contain either a single IP or a dual-stack pair of IPs")
+	} else if len(nodeIPs) == 2 && !allowDual {
+		return nil, fmt.Errorf("dual-stack not supported in this configuration")
+	} else if len(nodeIPs) == 2 && (nodeIPs[0].IsUnspecified() || nodeIPs[1].IsUnspecified()) {
+		return nil, fmt.Errorf("dual-stack node IP cannot include '0.0.0.0' or '::'")
+	}
+
+	return nodeIPs, nil
+}
+
+// ParseNodeIPArgument parses kubelet's --node-ip argument. If nodeIP contains invalid
+// values, they will be logged and ignored. Dual-stack node IPs are allowed if
+// cloudProvider is unset, or if it is `"external"` and allowCloudDualStack is true.
+func ParseNodeIPArgument(nodeIP, cloudProvider string, allowCloudDualStack bool) ([]net.IP, error) {

There are cases where it's useful for a pod to know its own owner.

This would need a KEP to argue the formatting and edge cases, but shouldn't be too hard.

xref https://github.com/gocrane/crane/issues/716

Talking to external users who rely on parsing the name, I am seeing a pattern of "the info I need isn't available, so I hacked around it" (my words, not theirs).

I'll spend some time thinking about how we could get the info they want into the places they want.  In particular their seems to be a desire to know the deployment name from the API (via labels) and inside the pod (env or downward API) and in metrics.

> How do they inject it then? I can't imagine anything other than a webhook, which can be changed to use the OwnerReferences.

Some of them parse the name as `([-a-z0-9]+)-([-a-z0-9]{10})-([-a-z0-9]{5}` and use the first match.  Some go farther and encode the actual character sets (2nd part being hex and 3rd part being the non-vowel-like aplhanum set).

Obviously this doesn't work for non-deployments, but some of these projects are only dealin with pods that *are* deployments, so it doesn't have to be truly general-purpose.

We could add well-known labels like `workload.kubernetes.io/{deployment,replicaset,statuefulset,job,...}` - this works for us, bt not for non-core workload controllers.  We'd have to verify that all of these APIs' object names are valid label values (hint: they are not), which makes it harder again.  We could hash the deployment name if it is too long or use the UID. All that tells you is that 2 pods are in the same deployment, not what the deployment name actually is.  

We could use annotations instead, which isn't selectable.

We could do a combination, so the info was available in full form.  But we know deployments can orphan and adopt pods, so it's still imperfect.  We can't just hash the selector, because an adoption could happen with a different-but-compatible selector.

So it needs more thought :)
/lgtm

I think there are a couple ways this could be approached, which need some consideration.  Off the top of my head:

1) Require that Deployment's name be less than 242 (253 - 10 for hash - 1 for '-') characters.  This seems like a breaking change, but no deployment with that name could ever actually WORK, so acceptable.

2) Accept long names but internally truncate to 242 when creating the replicaset(s).  This leaves room for the replicaset suffix, but that will again be truncated when the pod is created with `generateName`, which only uses 63 characters.  This already generates a warning, so maybe OK?  This will break anyone who tries to parse the RS name and extract the deployment name.

3) Accept long names but internally truncate to fit `generateName` (e.g. 46 (63 - 10 for hash - 1 for '-' - 6 for pod random suffix) when creating the replicaset(s). This will break anyone who tries to parse the RS name and extract the deployment name.

4) Just issue a warning when the name will result in failure (I dislike this option).

No matter what you do, there's a potential for clients who depend on undefined behavior.  For example, they parse out the last `-.{5}` of the pod name to get the replicaset name, and the last `-.{10}` to get the deployment name.  For very long names they will be broken, but they kind of are broken already.

There are almost certainly other options that make different/better tradeoffs, too!

comment_text:
This pattern of DeepCopy and change could also be cleaned up (different commits or PRs).  You could do:

```
newSvc = copyService(oldSvc, tweakAppProtocol("http"))
```

or

```
newSvc = tweakAppProtocol("http")(oldSvd.DeepCopy())
```

but I like the first one better.

I keep snoozing this for two weeks at a time, hoping SOMEONE will come back to it.

I feel very strongly that https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/100277#issuecomment-929522504 is an incorrect assessment.  It's not "the possibility that the cached status is wrong and a pod will be marked as ready when it's not" vs. "the delay in marking the pod as ready".  It's about the act of changing a pod from ready to unready without a justified reason.  "I don't know" is not more trustworthy than "let it ride".  In fact, it's less material.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/100277#issuecomment-929549329 is correct to me.

I verified that this is still the case (not quite HEAD, but a few weeks stale build).

I don't know what the "breaking change" alluded to would be, but I keep hearing about this case from people.  IMO we need to fix it.

/cc @mrunalp @SergeyKanzhelev @bobbypage @derekwaynecarr
This doesn't verify that the input was valid, it just chooses not to ignore it.  Do we validate somewhere else that it was correct?
How did this go almost a year without being hit?

```$ k get -o yaml pod iamyouare-5849d6b47-gg9sl | kubectl create -f -
Error from server (AlreadyExists): error when creating "STDIN": pods "iamyouare-5849d6b47-gg9sl" already exists, the server was not able to generate a unique name for the object

$ k get -o yaml service iamyouare | kubectl create -f -
The Service "iamyouare" is invalid: spec.clusterIPs: Invalid value: []string{"10.0.176.164"}: failed to allocate IP 10.0.176.164: provided IP is already allocated
```

That is fun.  Even if I force a validation error in the pod, I get "AlreadyExists".  I don't know exactly why, except that Service uses some uncommon paths through the apiserver.  Digging a bit...

staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go `func (e *Store) Create()` calls `e.BeginCreate()` which is what is failing in this allocation.  We don't know until much later if the name already exists, but I don't know where, exactly.

Later in Create() we call `BeforeCreate()` which calls validation.  If validation fails, we can get the same sort of error on pods:

```
$ k get -o yaml pod iamyouare-5849d6b47-gg9sl \
    | sed 's/restartPolicy:.*/restartPolicy: foobar/' \
    | sed 's/generateName:.*//' \
    | kubectl --validate=false create -f -
The Pod "iamyouare-5849d6b47-gg9sl" is invalid: spec.restartPolicy: Unsupported value: "foobar": supported values: "Always", "OnFailure", "Never"
```

So this is not unique to Service.

@deads2k @lavalamp 
/sig api-machinery
@ibabou Is the file you are pointing to a UNIX-style resolv.conf or something else?
`getDNSConfig()` handles it internally

Is "Host" sufficiently unambiguous now?  For example, could I point this flag at a file named "File" ?  If so, why would "Host" not mean a file named "Host" ?

Is there some syntax Windows comprehends that is clear not a possible filename?  "Host:" or "host://" or something without the ambiguity?

Or can we assert that this must be an absolute path?  Does a relative path like "File" work today?  We didn't used to assert that, so it's kind of a breaking change.

I'd say to give this one a day or so before aborting the previous PR

> sure, If I understand correctly then this pr will end up with following commits
> 
> 1. change the original test to tweak mode
> 2. add a new service tweak method
> 3. add the changes related to node tweaks in #3d63aed2
> 4. remove duplicate tests
> 5. whitespace cleanups
>
> Maybe 2 & 3 could be merged?

Yes those can merge.  I'm not really asking you to go back and do the following, because it's tedious, but the ideal form of this PR would be:

1. Add tweaks to `newService()`, add `tweakAddETP()`, get rid of `newETPLocalService()`, replace all callers of `newETPLocalService` with the tweak.
2. Add `tweakAddLBIngress()`, fixup all possible cases to use it
3. Add `makeServicePort` and `tweakAddPorts`, fuxup all possible cases to use it
4. repeat for `tweakAddLBClass`, `tweakAddFinalizers`, `tweakAddDeletionTimestamp`, ...
5. remove duplicate tests
6. whitespace cleanups

You would end up with something like 8 commits.  So for any FUTURE work, I think it's worthwhile to think about how to get the smallest, easiest to verify commits.
OK - the goal is "as obvious as possible, and not one step further".  If you think that's the right tradeoff, go for it.

how about `"abc" + strings.Repeat("-123456789", 25)` - far easier to read and verify.  For `expected` it would be `"abc" + strings.Repeat("-123456789", 23) + "-12345678-" + hash` I guess.

Or even easier:  `strings.repeat("x", 253)` and `strings.Repeat("x", 242) + "-" + hash` ?
I'd also add testcases for input of exactly 242 (no truncation), 243 (1 tructation) and 252 (9 truncation).
/cc @liggitt @lavalamp - FYI.  This sort of goes to the issues with generateName and people parsing names.

>> Was there a discussion around this?

Asking again - was there a SIG discussion around this?  Or a KEP maybe?  I edited https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/116447#issuecomment-1475040771 a bit - personally I kind of prefer option 3, but I'd like SIG Apps to own the decision.

> Have a situation that need for parse pod name to get deployment name and replicaset name?

To be clear: I think parsing pod names is unworkable from the beginning.  But people still do it.  That's a bigger issue and you don't need to solve it here.  It's worth calling out in the release notes for this PR, though.  Something like:

"""
Previously, Deployments with names longer than 242 characters would be accepted, but would fail to create ReplicaSets, and therefore fail to create Pods.  Now, such Deployments will be accepted and the name will be truncated when creating ReplicaSets.  Deployments with names longer than 57 characters will result in Pods whose names are truncated to accomodate a random suffix, as before.
"""
Yeah, it can't merge until the 1.28 window opens, but that's OK.  It's ready to go and it seems unlikely to grow a conflict in this area.

"Deprecated" doesn't mean "will be deleted".  It just means "don't do new things with this".

I think that calling it deprecated is appropriate, though I am OK to remove the scary "will be removed" part.

How about:

"""
Deprecated: This field was under-specified and its meaning varies across implementations.  Using it is non-portable and it may not support dual-stack.  Users are encouraged to use implementation-specific annotations when available.
"""

>> 1. Require that Deployment's name be less than 242 (253 - 10 for hash - 1 for '-') characters.
>
> This seems like the best solution to me as we would not break any working deployments.

This is the least impactful change, but I think it's still less than ideal.  Just to be really clear, even ignoring the outright failure mode I reported initially, it is bad in other cases:

What happens today with a long name:
* Create deployment `<59 chars name>`
* Creates RS `<59 chars name>-<10 char hash>`
* Creates pods `<58 chars (truncated)><5 char random>` - note, the hash is totally gone

It's impossible to parse that into either Deployment name or ReplicaSet name.  Both have been obscured.  Worse, if you happen to have 2 long-name Deployments which are the same initial 58 characters, the pods are indistinguishable by name.

But also:
* Create deployment `<48 chars name>`
* Creates RS `<48 chars name>-<10 char hash>`
* Creates pods `<48 chars name>-<9 char hash (truncated)><5 char random>`

At 48 characters, the deployment name survives, but the hash is obscured, so RS name is lost.

This is why I sort of lean to option 3, personally - the pod name includes the whole RS name, but the RS name is a truncated form of the deployment.  I think that's a better tradeoff because humans look at pod names more than RS names, and grouping matters, so optimizing for the better pod names is better.  But I can sort of buy the argument for option 1 too.

I checked out your branch and ran ./hack/update-openapi-spec.sh and it made a lot of changes.

It's not clear to me why this PR is adding a bunch of files.

I squashed it down and re-ran the updates (`./hack/update-codegen.sh swagger openapi proto;  ./hack/update-openapi-spec.sh`) and got a very different result than what you have in this PR.

I would never have guessed that it was lack of tags causing this sort of problem.  I always just `git fetch upstream master` and then rebase to that.

/lgtm
/approve

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/40610#issuecomment-1231988005

KEPs are welcome!

> Nevertheless this would only get fixed for deployments. Anyone creating replicas manually or by other means, will break the consistency. So you cannot be sure that you can always pair the Pod back to RS

This is true - we can only fix the parts we control, and we don't have a general "append to the name" model because it's complex to design something really useful and because parsing the name is (in general) an anti-pattern.  We know that a pod could be orphaned from an RS/Deployment and adopted by another.  At that point, the pod's name is a staight-up LIE.  :)

It's unfortunate that Deployment becomes aware of RelicaSet's implementation details (i.e. if RS didn't use `generateName`, we'd be having a different discussion!).  Ideally, Deployment would just make totally random RS names, and RS would make totally random pod names, and nobody would parse them - not in code and not visually.  But that's a crappy human UX for the most common path.

`pushd` will fail and trigger errexit.  `go list` operates on what IS there, rather than what should be there (unlike git ls-files).  So assuming that `go list` produced an output, that output should exist (unless we lost a race against someone else removing it at the same instant), and so pushd failing and aborting seems OK.

Really, if we want the best possible results, we would check the errorcode of EVERY COMMAND, but who wants to do that? :)

I concur with @jpbetz and while I know it's not what you want to hear, I'd appreciate it if you take a less accusatory tone.

Snap defining $HOME to mean something other than its normal meaning is weird and broken.  LOTS of software looks at $HOME, and almost none looks at $SNAP_REAL_HOME.  If some critical mass of users relied on snap  day-to-day, maybe.  But as it stands, they don't seem to be in a position to demand that the world adapt to their bizarro conventions.

I don't object to checking `user.Current()` for `.HomeDir` - but I would check that AFTER looking for $HOME - anything else is a potentially breaking change.

I could also see an argument to look for $XDG_CONFIG_HOME (that's at least somewhat standard), but again *after* looking for $HOME.  

Crawling up the directory tree is pretty sketchy, and I don't think we want to entertain that idea.

I can't speak to OpenStack's decisions - we are not them.  If some massive body of users chimes in saying that this is a pain point for them, I'll be happy to re-evaluate.

comment_text:
yes, the space is needed to produce the right output.


As you raised above, there is a POTENTIAL whitespace issue, but not an ACTUAL issue - all the places we do this are controlled inputs which do not include the user-owned path.  They are all either Go package names (as returned by `go list` - no spaces) or binary names enumerated immediately before.  So One option is to leave it as printf. 

Exploring:

```
$ # foo just prints info about its args
$ function foo() { local i=1; for arg; do echo "\$$i: $arg"; i=$((i+1)); done; }

$ foo a b "c d e"
$1: a
$2: b
$3: c d e

$ X=("a" "b" "c d e")

$ printf -- "--arg=\"%s\" " "${X[@]}"; echo;
--arg="a" --arg="b" --arg="c d e" 
```

That looks OK, but...

```
$ foo $(printf -- "--arg=\"%s\" " "${X[@]}")
$1: --arg="a"
$2: --arg="b"
$3: --arg="c
$4: d
$5: e"
```

The shell evaluation of quoted strings doesn't survive.  We could do:

```
$ eval foo $(printf -- "--arg=\"%s\" " "${X[@]}")
$1: --arg=a
$2: --arg=b
$3: --arg=c d e
```

...but that is easy to misuse or just forget about.  Bash arrays and functions are a mess, which is why we have things like read-array which is deeeeep dark magic

or else we take it into our own hands:

```
$ A=(); for arg in "${X[@]}"; do A+=("--arg=\"$arg\""); done

$ foo "${A[@]}"
$1: --arg="a"
$2: --arg="b"
$3: --arg="c d e"
```

It's not so bad, but it can't be a function.

Another option is to use the IFS trick (`(IFS=,; foo "${X[*]}")` - which, while terse, is also a bit brittle (IFS has to be set on a distinct command and `[@]` does not work).  This passes a single `--input-dirs=foo,bar,bat` rather than `--input-dirs=foo --input-dirs=bar --input-dirs=bat`.

I'll add commits in a couple styles - you tell me which you think make sense, and then I will back-integrate :)

@@ -0,0 +1,585 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+
+# Copyright 2023 The Kubernetes Authors.
+#
+# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
+# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
+# You may obtain a copy of the License at
+#
+#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
+#
+# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
+# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
+# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
+# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
+# limitations under the License.
+
+# shellcheck disable=2046 # printf word-splitting is intentional
+
+# This presents several functions for packages which want to use kubernetes
+# code-generation tools.
+
+set -o errexit
+set -o nounset
+set -o pipefail
+
+KUBE_CODEGEN_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd -P)"
+
+function kube::codegen::internal::git_find() {
+    # Similar to find but faster and easier to understand.  We want to include
+    # modified and untracked files because this might be running against code
+    # which is not tracked by git yet.
+    git ls-files -cmo --exclude-standard "$@"
+}
+
+function kube::codegen::internal::git_grep() {
+    # We want to include modified and untracked files because this might be
+    # running against code which is not tracked by git yet.
+    git grep --untracked "$@"
+}
+
+# Generate tagged helper code: conversions, deepcopy, and defaults
+#
+# Args:
+#   --input-pkg-root <string>
+#     The root package under which to search for files which request code to be
+#     generated.  This must be Go package syntax, e.g.  "k8s.io/foo/bar".
+#
+#   --output-base <string>
+#     The root directory under which to emit code.  The concatenation of
+#     <output-base> + <input-pkg-root> must be valid.
+#
+#   --boilerplate <string = path_to_kube_codegen_boilerplate>
+#     An optional override for the header file to insert into generated files.
+#
+function kube::codegen::gen_helpers() {
+    local in_pkg_root=""
+    local out_base="" # gengo needs the output dir must be $out_base/$out_pkg_root
+    local boilerplate="${KUBE_CODEGEN_ROOT}/hack/boilerplate.go.txt"
+    local v="${KUBE_VERBOSE:-0}"
+
+    while [ "$#" -gt 0 ]; do
+        case "$1" in
+            "--input-pkg-root")
+                in_pkg_root="$2"
+                shift 2
+                ;;
+            "--output-base")
+                out_base="$2"
+                shift 2
+                ;;
+            "--boilerplate")
+                boilerplate="$2"
+                shift 2
+                ;;
+            *)
+                echo "unknown argument: $1" >&2
+                return 1
+                ;;
+        esac
+    done
+
+    if [ -z "${in_pkg_root}" ]; then
+        echo "--input-pkg-root is required" >&2
+        return 1
+    fi
+    if [ -z "${out_base}" ]; then
+        echo "--output-base is required" >&2
+        return 1
+    fi
+
+    (
+        # To support running this from anywhere, first cd into this directory,
+        # and then install with forced module mode on and fully qualified name.
+        cd "${KUBE_CODEGEN_ROOT}"
+        BINS=(
+            conversion-gen
+            deepcopy-gen
+            defaulter-gen
+        )
+        # shellcheck disable=2046 # printf word-splitting is intentional
+        GO111MODULE=on go install $(printf "k8s.io/code-generator/cmd/%s " "${BINS[@]}")

```release-note
NONE
```

The primary requirement is that it has to run from the root of each module (standalone) and from k/k.

Options:

1) Put it in a subdir of each module
2) Put it in a dotfile of each module
3) pass `--report-filename` as an arg to each module's update-codegen.sh
4) use a hidden env var to pass the moral equivalent of `--report-filename` to each module
5) try to sniff up the directory tree and figure out if we are running from k/k or not

(1) and (2) are OK to me.  The rest all kind of stink.  We simulataneously want these repos to work as standalone things AND we want them to dump their report into a parent which might not exist.

If I am squeezed, I will suggest #3, but I'll have to hold me nose while I do it :)

We could do #1 with OWNERS ?

comment_text:
If you look at
https://github.com/kubernetes/code-generator/blob/master/examples/go.mod you can see that it includes a block like:

```
replace (
	k8s.io/api => ../../api
	k8s.io/apimachinery => ../../apimachinery
	k8s.io/client-go => ../../client-go
)
```

For most of our staging repos, there's just the one go.mod, which the publishing bot (I think?) fixes as it publishes, turning those relative paths into real versions.  But these sub-modules don't get processed. We have 2 such.

This commit makes update-vendor.sh handle them.  It's unclear if the publishing bot will reify the `replace` statements.

/kind bug

```release-note
NONE
```

Relaying for a customer, but it seems reasonable to me.

Could we get a `--service-account` flag on `kubectl create deployment` ?  It's not terribly common to use multiple SAs, but I am seeing it more and more, and it makes me happy.

Fix update-vendor.sh logging

Previously it would corrupt the log when it ran stuff like:
    go mod tidy >> "${LOG_FILE}" 2>&1
because this would reopen the file.  Also, if that failed, the `finish`
function would be called ALSO with output to the log.

Now we let &1 and &2 always be the log, and &11 and &22 are the real
stdout/stderr, which means we have to say that explicitly when we want
output.

No, I cannot do `OUT="&11"` - I would have to use `eval` to make that
work.

+    shift

+    go run ./cmd/gotemplate <hack/golangci.yaml.in >"${out}" "$@"

+generate hack/golangci.yaml Base=1
+generate hack/golangci-strict.yaml Strict=1
+generate hack/golangci-hints.yaml Hints=1
+
+# Inform about differences.
+echo
+diff -c hack/golangci.yaml hack/golangci-strict.yaml || true

comment_text:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/118725 illustrates some surprising behavior with `kubectl apply`.  I have found, repeatedly, that humans are bad at predicting what apply will do (made worse by client-side and server-side being different).

From talking to a lot of people, the `apply` action feels like it means `create-or-replace`, but it's really not.  It's a much more sophisticated resource co-ownership system.  If you don't keep that in mind, it will almost certainly NOT do what you expect it to do, which has led to many bugs over the years (as @apelisse can attest).

In hindsight, I wonder if "apply" was the right name for this.  Perhaps we really want 2 distinct operations - something like "kubectl set" (simple create-or-replace) and "kubectl merge" (what we call "apply" today).  I suspect that vast majority of issues reported with "apply" would be avoided because humans would mostly use "set", and "merge" would be much more clearly a variant of patch, left for "advanced" cases.

Saying `kubectl set -f deploy.yaml` intuitively means one thing, while `kubectl merge -f deploy.yaml` clearly means something else.  I don't know if we want to extend that to `edit`, too.

I wonder if it is possible to steer towards a future where this is clearer.  @apelisse @soltysh @KnVerey @eddiezane @jpbetz
should we denote this as "00fc" to make it clear?  It took me a minute to figure out what was missing here
These lists are supposed to be alphabetized.  Can you insert it in the right place?  I know not everything is correct.

+package util
+
+import (
+	"bytes"
+	"fmt"
+)
+
+// LineBuffer is an interface for writing lines of input to a bytes.Buffer
+type LineBuffer interface {
+	// Write takes a list of arguments, each a string or []string, joins all the
+	// individual strings with spaces, terminates with newline, and writes them to the
+	// buffer. Any other argument type will panic.
+	Write(args ...interface{})
+
+	// WriteBytes writes bytes to the buffer, and terminates with newline.
+	WriteBytes(bytes []byte)
+
+	// Reset clears the buffer
+	Reset()
+
+	// Bytes returns the contents of the buffer as a []byte
+	Bytes() []byte
+
+	// String returns the contents of the buffer as a string
+	String() string
+
+	// Lines returns the number of lines in the buffer. Note that more precisely, this
+	// returns the number of times Write() or WriteBytes() was called; it assumes that
+	// you never wrote any newlines to the buffer yourself.
+	Lines() int
+}
+
+type realLineBuffer struct {
+	b     bytes.Buffer
+	lines int
+}
+
+// NewLineBuffer returns a new "real" LineBuffer
+func NewLineBuffer() LineBuffer {
+	return &realLineBuffer{}
+}
+
+// Write is part of LineBuffer
+func (buf *realLineBuffer) Write(args ...interface{}) {
+	for i, arg := range args {
+		if i > 0 {
+			buf.b.WriteByte(' ')
+		}
+		switch x := arg.(type) {
+		case string:
+			buf.b.WriteString(x)
+		case []string:
+			for j, s := range x {
+				if j > 0 {
+					buf.b.WriteByte(' ')
+				}
+				buf.b.WriteString(s)
+			}
+		default:
+			panic(fmt.Sprintf("unknown argument type: %T", x))
+		}
+	}
+	buf.b.WriteByte('\n')
+	buf.lines++
+}
+
+// WriteBytes is part of LineBuffer
+func (buf *realLineBuffer) WriteBytes(bytes []byte) {
+	buf.b.Write(bytes)
+	buf.b.WriteByte('\n')
+	buf.lines++
+}
+
+// Reset is part of LineBuffer
+func (buf *realLineBuffer) Reset() {
+	buf.b.Reset()
+	buf.lines = 0
+}
+
+// Bytes is part of LineBuffer
+func (buf *realLineBuffer) Bytes() []byte {
+	return buf.b.Bytes()
+}
+
+// String is part of LineBuffer
+func (buf *realLineBuffer) String() string {
+	return buf.b.String()
+}
+
+// Lines is part of LineBuffer
+func (buf *realLineBuffer) Lines() int {
+	return buf.lines
+}
+
+type dummyLineBuffer struct {
+	lines int
+}
+
+// NewDummyLineBuffer returns a dummy LineBuffer that counts the number of writes but
+// throws away the data.

If we keep the stored value as `int32` then we should either force callers to pass `int32` or else keep this range check, shouldn't we?  I know we said "It is your responsibility..." but that is pretty unsatisfying.

So we have several options, most of which you have PR'ed now :)

1) Convert all callers to `FromInt32()` and cast.
2) Convert `FromInt()` to receive `int32` and make all callers cast.
3) Accept all signed ints <= 32 bits, for `int` callers to cast.
4) Change the stored value to int64.

Let's think about deserialization into fields of this type.

If I pass a number which is too big (e.g. 8B) I get an error, either complaioning about "-589934592" (forced cast client-side) or better, an unmarshal error: "json: cannot unmarshal number 8000000000 into Go struct field ServicePort.spec.ports.port of type int32".  Which error you get depends on whether you come in from `update` or `apply`/`patch`.

@apelisse @jpbetz  in case you care about this corner case - basically anywhere we use int32 we have inconsistent deserialization of out of range values.

I know I encouraged you to make this PR increasingly "simple" but I now wonder what's "safe"?  There are a few non-test places that call `intstr.Parse()` with user input, so the stack dump is probably better than silent corruption (considering that I can feed valid int64 values that alias int32.

Even if we convert to int64 (which should be OK because protobuf `varint`) we only push the problem out.  I ACK that someone has to be trying to make trouble to do this (e.g. I can set Deployment replicas to 8 quadrillion, which aliases to a valid int32, but I think that is "GIGO").

So maybe your original PR is actually better?  It doesn't fix deserialization, but it does force explicit callers to at least own their own range. 

#117631 will require callers who take user-input to pre-validate the range

#119285 has some nice updates to `intstr.Parse()`

We have the "downward API" which extracts almost exclusively information which is represented as fields on "this pod" and projects either env vars or files on disk. In https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/73760#issuecomment-790974166 (more than 2 years ago!) I suggested people use a sidecar to get and format the info.  We have never actually made such a sidecar image, leaving it as an exercise for the reader.

```
while true; do

done
```

OK.  We've spent too much tim eon the mechanics of this PR, so I am going to say I am convinced that your original approach (explict int32 API, callers need to bounds-check) is OK except for the serialization discussion, which I will recap here.

@skitt do you want to consolidate all the tweaks into that original PR and close the rest?

@apelisse @jpbetz @deads2k @liggitt @smarterclayton @wojtek-t  @msau42 

Long ago, we chose to encode numeric fields in the API as `int32` to be clear about the size (vs `int` which is platform-defined).  Overall this has been fine, except for a few places where we didn't properly bounds-check for negatives (oops) and now zap those to zero and/or issue warnings.  We have a bunch of other fields that are encoded as `int64`, presumably for the same reasons.

The issue comes in with (almost certainly intentional) abuse of API.  E.g. I can set a Deployment's replicas to 8 quadrillion, which aliases to a valid int32, but is clearly wrong.  We don't bounds-check that value.  Basically, as we deserialize from wire-format into int32, we can either lose/corrupt information or we can trigger errors in deserialization.  Those errors are not our usual style of API errors, which is unfortunate.  This is the same reason we transport IP adresses as `string`, so we can deserialize them and produce consistent errors. 

If we had encoded all numeric fields as `int64`, the valid range would be much larger, whihc would allow us more opportunity to catch bad inputs, but doesn't solve for cases like above, where you can legitimately set replicas to values in the billions, and we don't range check. 

Do we care?  If you provide a ridiculously large value that causes int32 or int64 aliasing, are we satisfied to call that "GIGO"?  The alternative would be to convert `int32` -> `int64` or even do more clever deserialization to preserve values.  I think that is not worth the effort, but I wanted to raise it so we all at least ACK the existence of the corner-case.

Lastly, should we encourage/require net new numeric fields to be int64 and to do range checking (e.g. 1M replicas should be enough for anyone) ?

The fact that the .status.loadBalancer field can be set while .spec.type
is not "LoadBalancer" is a flub.  Any spec update will already clear

change, updates which try to set this combination will fail validation.

Existing cases of this will not be broken.  Any spec/metadata update
will clear it (no error) and this is the only stanza of status.

New gate "AllowServiceLBStatusOnNonLB" is off by default, but can be
enabled if this change actually breaks someone, which seems exceeedingly
unlikely.

Fixes #119700

The fact that the .status.loadBalancer field can be set while .spec.type is not "LoadBalancer" is a flub.  Any spec update will already clear .status.ingress, so it's hard to really rely on this.  After this change, updates which try to set this combination will fail validation.

Existing cases of this will not be broken.  Any spec/metadata update will clear it (no error) and this is the only stanza of status.

New gate "AllowServiceLBStatusOnNonLB" is off by default, but can be enabled if this change actually breaks someone, which seems exceeedingly unlikely.


/kind bug

```release-note
Setting the `status.loadBalancer` of a Service whose `spec.type` is not `"LoadBalancer"` was previously allowed, but any update to the `metadata` or `spec` would wipe that field. Setting this field is no longer permitted unless `spec.type` is  `"LoadBalancer"`.  In the very unlikely event that this has unexpected impact, you can enable the `AllowServiceLBStatusOnNonLB` feature gate, which will restore the previous behavior.  If you do need to set this, please file an issue with the Kubernetes project to help contributors understand why you need it.
```

Ahh, I see.  For TCP, we have default values which we assert.  So we need to know the difference between "uspecified" (meaning "set the default value") and "specified as 0" (meaning "do not change").

We're not proposing default values for these new ones, so 0, whether specified or not, means "do not change it".  Right?

And setting a default at this point is likely to end in tears for someone.

So adding the nil-check is actually wrong - the TCP fields *must* have a value by this point, and allowing nil to slip through is actually bad.

Can you please remove those nil-checks and add comments like "config.TCPEstablishedTimeout has a default value, so can't be nil"?

We have lots of validation which checks mutually-incompatible fields or dependent fields.  E.g. errors of the form "X: must not be set when Y is set" or "X: may only be set when Y is Z".  The error strings are all over the map.  I think we should encapsulate those into a few functions, like `field.Invalid` to make them easier and more consistent.

e.g. `field.IncompatibleWith(thisPath, otherPath)` or `field.DependsOnValue(thisPath, otherPath, someConstant)`

This does not eliminate the need to CHECK the fields, but it makes the errors better.

Someone can rack up a lot of commits by doing this carefully.  I'd do it as:
* 1 commit to add the functions and tests
* 1 commit per function or small-group of related functions converted to use these (and test cases!)

My WIP if someone wants to jump on this https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/compare/master...thockin:kubernetes:validate_field_incompatible_errors?expand=1

I am sure that these are not sufficient for all cases, but will get a lot.

+// NamespacedBy returns the namespace of the Linux kernel for a sysctl, or

+func NamespacedBy(val string) Namespace {
+	if ns, found := namespaces[val]; found {
+		return ns
+	}
+	for p, ns := range prefixNamespaces {
+		if strings.HasPrefix(val, p) {
+			return ns
+		}
+	}
+	return UnknownNamespace
+}
+
+// GetNamespace extracts information from a sysctl string. It returns:
+//  1. The sysctl namespace, which can be one of the following: IPC, Net, or unknown.

+//     If there is no '*' suffix, it will be an empty string.

comment_text:
Fix "list_type_missing" API violations in meta/v1

This assumes that any such field is atomic, except:
  * OwnerReferences: because it has a `+patchStrategy=merge`, but it
    probably needs a `+listMapKey=...` ?
  * Finalizers: because it hs a `+patchStrategy=merge`, but is a
    primitive type (string).
  * []byte fields, which should not be failing this anyway (fixed
    subsequently).

An alternative approach could be just to turn off the API warnings for
these fields, but it felt more correct to declare the semantics.

+	command := NewLoggerCommand()
+	code := cli.Run(command)
+	os.Exit(code)
+}
+
+func NewLoggerCommand() *cobra.Command {
+	c := logsapi.NewLoggingConfiguration()
+	cmd := &cobra.Command{
+		Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) {
+			// This configures the global logger in klog *and* slog, if compiled
+			// with Go >= 1.21.
+			logs.InitLogs()
+			if err := logsapi.ValidateAndApply(c, featureGate); err != nil {
+				fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "%v\n", err)
+				os.Exit(1)
+			}
+			if len(args) > 0 {
+				fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "Unexpected additional command line arguments:\n    %s\n", strings.Join(args, "\n    "))
+				os.Exit(1)
+			}
+
+			// Produce some output. Special types used by Kubernetes work.
+			podRef := klog.KObj(&metav1.ObjectMeta{Name: "some-pod", Namespace: "some-namespace"})
+			podRefs := klog.KObjSlice([]interface{}{
+				&metav1.ObjectMeta{Name: "some-pod", Namespace: "some-namespace"},
+				nil,
+				&metav1.ObjectMeta{Name: "other-pod"},
+			})
+			slog.Info("slog.Info", "pod", podRef, "pods", podRefs)
+			klog.InfoS("klog.Info", "pod", podRef, "pods", podRefs)

+func main() {
+	options := slog.HandlerOptions{AddSource: true}
+	textHandler := slog.NewTextHandler(os.Stderr, &options)
+	textLogger := slog.New(textHandler)
+
+	// Use text output as default logger.
+	slog.SetDefault(textLogger)
+
+	// This also needs to be done through klog to ensure that all code
+	// using klog uses the text handler. klog.Background/TODO/FromContext
+	// will return a thin wrapper around the textHandler, so all that klog
+	// still does is manage the global default and retrieval from contexts.
+	klog.SetLoggerWithOptions(slogr.NewLogr(textHandler), klog.ContextualLogger(true))
+
+	textLogger.Info("slog.Logger.Info")
+	klog.InfoS("klog.Info")

comment_text:
From an end-user POV, making them pass a `logr.Logger` when then they want to use `slog.Logger` (or `slog.Handler`) is kind of rude.  "I use slog because that is the new Go hotness, and I use Kubernetes.  What is this logr noise you're making me deal with?"

Why not offer klog interfaces to point kubernetes logging at either logr or slog (which could be implemented as `logr.FromSlogHandler()`.  Make the poor users do as little cut-paste as possible.

Make build scripts use Go import paths

This helps 2 things:
1) output is cleaner
2) whether a build is static or non-static depends on string matching,
   which is easier after this

If you specify a `...` pattern, this will preserve it, which breaks the
static/non-static matching but really, can we just agree that `make` is
for binaries?  It works for other things but so does `go build`.

Example:

```
$ make WHAT="cmd/kubectl/ ./staging/src/k8s.io/api/... ./test/e2e/e2e.test ./test/e2e/e2e.test ./cmd/kubectl/ "
go version go1.21.4 linux/amd64

    k8s.io/api/... (non-static)
    k8s.io/kubernetes/cmd/kubectl (static)
    k8s.io/kubernetes/test/e2e/e2e.test (test)
```

+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+
+# Copyright 2022 The Kubernetes Authors.
+#
+# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
+# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
+# You may obtain a copy of the License at
+#
+#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
+#
+# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
+# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
+# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
+# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
+# limitations under the License.
+
+# This script generates go.work so that it includes all Go packages
+# in this repo, with a few exceptions.
+
+set -o errexit
+set -o nounset
+set -o pipefail
+
+# Go tools really don't like it if you have a symlink in `pwd`.
+cd "$(pwd -P)"
+
+KUBE_ROOT=$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/..
+source "${KUBE_ROOT}/hack/lib/init.sh"
+
+# This sets up the environment, like GOCACHE, which keeps the worktree cleaner.
+kube::golang::setup_env
+export GO111MODULE=on # TODO(thockin): remove this when it's the default
+
+cd "${KUBE_ROOT}"
+
+# First nuke the top-level workspace files.  We have to do this for a few
+# steps.  They will be recreated later.
+rm -f go.work go.work.sum
+
+# Treat hack/tools as a distinct workspace, to prevent tooling from trying to
+# merge deps between it and the top-level workspace.  This block has to go
+# first because `go work` doesn't know how to deal with nested workspaces
+# ("go: .../go.work already exists").
+# https://github.com/golang/go/issues/53938
+(
+    cd hack/tools
+    rm -f go.work go.work.sum
+    go work init .
+    X=$(cat go.work)
+    cat > go.work << __EOF__
+// This is a hack, but it prevents go from climbing further and trying to
+// reconcile the various deps across the "real" modules and this one.
+
+$X
+__EOF__
+    go mod download # generate go.work.sum
+)
+
+# Generate the top-level workspace.
+go work init
+git ls-files -z ':(glob)**/go.mod' \
+    | xargs -0 -n1 dirname \
+    | grep -v "/_" ` # skip directories named _foo ` \

I am working on the Go workspaces effort this week, and hit a snag.

We have a few `go.mod` which are supposed to insulate their parents from deps.  Specifically:

./hack/tools/go.mod
./staging/src/k8s.io/kms/internal/plugins/_mock/go.mod
./staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/examples/go.mod

Tools I get, and I handle it.   I don't add it to the workspace.  Fortunately, no real code in there.

KMS README says it is named _mock, so that tooling ignores it, but it's REALLY unclear to me why we want that?  At the time of this writing it doesn't actually build, so clearly it's not used?  @nilekhc @enj ?

The code-gen examples are more challenging.  If I include them from the workspace, then it wants to update vendor to cover their deps.  If I exclude them, then running all the codegen tools needs to be done twice - once for `tool ./...` and once for `(cd staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/examples; tool ./...)`

Simpler, maybe is just to drop those go.mod files?  This pulls a few new deps into k/k (github.com/ThalesIgnite/crypto11,
github.com/miekg/pkcs11, github.com/thales-e-security/pool) but several more into k8s.io/code-generator (most of which were already managed in k/k).

Let's see what folks think (and what CI thinks).

@liggitt

KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/4402

The main Kubernetes git repo (github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes, colloquially
called "k/k") is the embodiment of evolution.  When it was created, the only
way to build Go applications was GOPATH.  Over time we built tooling which
understood GOPATH, and it leaked into many parts of our build, test, and
release systems.  Then Go added modules, in part because GOPATH was unpleasant
and had a tendency to leak into tools.  We adopted modules, but we also added
the idea of "staging" repositories - a way to eat our cake and have, too.  We
get the benefits of a monorepo (atomic commits, fast iteration across repos)
and then publish those to standalone repos for downstream consumption.  To make
this work with modules, we abused GOPATH even harder and wrote even more tools.

The Go project saw what we (and others) were doing and did not like it.  So
they created [workspaces](https://go.dev/doc/tutorial/workspaces).  They
basically created a solution that is purpose-built for us.

Let's use it.

One major aspect of this is code-generation.  It's FAR simpler now, but how does it perform?  After factoring out build time (by doing a full build first) we can see it has little impact:

before:
```
$ time ./hack/update-codegen.sh protobuf deepcopy prerelease defaults conversions openapi applyconfigs clients listers informers
```

EOL code-generator/generate-groups scripts

There's no easy way to make it work in the new model.  Callers add flags
to the commandline like:

```
"${CODEGEN_PKG}/generate-internal-groups.sh" "client,conversion,deepcopy,defaulter,informer,lister,openapi" \
  k8s.io/code-generator/examples/apiserver \
  k8s.io/code-generator/examples/apiserver/apis \
  k8s.io/code-generator/examples/apiserver/apis \
  "example:v1 example2:v1 example3.io:v1" \
  --output-base "${SCRIPT_DIR}/../../../.." \
  --go-header-file "${SCRIPT_DIR}/boilerplate.go.txt"
```

To support this we would need to parse these flags in these scripts and
try to adapt them to the new CLI tool interfaces.  Not impossible, but
seeing as we deprecated these a few releases ago AND that we have other
breaking changes to the tools, let's just rip this bandaid off?
Move import-boss: k/code-generator/cmd -> k/k/cmd

When I restructured how boilerplate is emitted, I did not preserve it.  Go has a convention, documented in `go help generate`:

```
To convey to humans and machine tools that code is generated,
generated source should have a line that matches the following
regular expression (in Go syntax):

        ^// Code generated .* DO NOT EDIT\.$
```

It was in the "code-generator" package before because it used gengo, even though it does not actually "generate" any "code".  I moved it back to k/k but I am ambivalent about it - does anyone else actually use it?  It's a very opinionated tool :)

Get rid of code-generator/cmd/openapi-gen

It's an exact duplicate of k8s.io/kube-openapi/cmd/openapi-gen.
It's the record of which releases had which stability

code_context:
@@ -0,0 +1,65 @@
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+
+# Copyright 2022 The Kubernetes Authors.
+#
+# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
+# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
+# You may obtain a copy of the License at
+#
+#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
+#
+# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
+# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
+# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
+# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
+# limitations under the License.
+
+# This script generates go.work so that it includes all Go packages
+# in this repo, with a few exceptions.
+
+set -o errexit
+set -o nounset
+set -o pipefail
+
+# Go tools really don't like it if you have a symlink in `pwd`.
+cd "$(pwd -P)"
+
+KUBE_ROOT=$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/..
+source "${KUBE_ROOT}/hack/lib/init.sh"
+
+# This sets up the environment, like GOCACHE, which keeps the worktree cleaner.
+kube::golang::setup_env
+export GO111MODULE=on # TODO(thockin): remove this when it's the default
+
+cd "${KUBE_ROOT}"
+
+# First nuke the top-level workspace files.  We have to do this for a few
+# steps.  They will be recreated later.
+rm -f go.work go.work.sum
+
+# Treat hack/tools as a distinct workspace, to prevent tooling from trying to
+# merge deps between it and the top-level workspace.  This block has to go
+# first because `go work` doesn't know how to deal with nested workspaces
+# ("go: .../go.work already exists").
+# https://github.com/golang/go/issues/53938
+(
+    cd hack/tools
+    rm -f go.work go.work.sum
+    go work init .
+    X=$(cat go.work)
+    cat > go.work << __EOF__
+// This is a hack, but it prevents go from climbing further and trying to
+// reconcile the various deps across the "real" modules and this one.
+
+$X
+__EOF__
+    go mod download # generate go.work.sum
+)
+
+# Generate the top-level workspace.
+go work init
+go work edit -use .
+git ls-files -z ':(glob)./staging/src/k8s.io/*/go.mod' \

```
RecursiveReadOnly specifies whether read-only mounts should be handled
recursively.

If ReadOnly is false, this field has no meaning and must be unspecified.

If ReadOnly is true, and this field is set to Disabled, the mount is not made
recursively read-only.  If this field is set to IfPossible, the mount is made
recursively read-only, if it is supported by the container runtime.  If this
field is set to Enabled, the mount is made recursively read-only if it is
supported by the container runtime, otherwise the pod will not be started and
an error will be generated to indicate the reason.

If this field is set to IfPossible or Enabled, MountPropagation must be set to
None (or be unspecified, which defaults to None).

If this field is not specified on a Pod it defaults to Disabled for backwards
compatibility.  If this field is not specified on an embedded PodSpec (e.g. in
a Deployment), it has no default value until a Pod is created, at which point
```

KEP: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/4402

This is a new PR based on #122624, to clear the lengthy history.

What is it?

The main Kubernetes git repo (github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes, colloquially called "k/k") is the embodiment of evolution.  When it was created, the only way to build Go applications was GOPATH.  Over time we built tooling which understood GOPATH, and it leaked into many parts of our build, test, and release systems.  Then Go added modules, in part because GOPATH was unpleasant and had a tendency to leak into tools.  We adopted modules, but we also added the idea of "staging" repositories - a way to eat our cake and have, too.  We get the benefits of a monorepo (atomic commits, fast iteration across repos) and then publish those to standalone repos for downstream consumption.  To make this work with modules, we abused GOPATH even harder and wrote even more tools.

The Go project saw what we (and others) were doing and did not like it.  So they created [workspaces](https://go.dev/doc/tutorial/workspaces).  They basically created a solution that is purpose-built for us.

Let's use it.

On reviewing:  Review each commit individually.  This PR was crafted in lock-step with a series of gengo commits, but since those are merged and gengo is in a different repo, we can't really see those any more.  This means that a lot of things don't work at each commit in this PR.  This is the only way this could be a sane PR to review though (if you call this sane).

I fully expect that once this merges we will find a long-tail of out-of-tree impacts (tests which need to use go 1.22, etc).

One major aspect of this is code-generation.  It's FAR simpler now, but how does it perform?  After factoring out build time (by doing a full build first) we can see it has little impact:

Should we acknowledge that this is a compat thing?  E.g.

"""
If specified, this must be an IANA_SVC_NAME.  Each named port in a container must have a unique name.  Due to backwards compatibility, ports on different containers in a pod may have the same name, in which case Probes and Services which refer to ports by name will use the first instance of that name.  Intentional use of this behavior is discouraged.
"""

I think we should still warn if ANY two containers use the same port name (which I think this will do, but should add tests to cover)

Seperately, we should consider if services and probes should hunt for ports by name inside initContainers (I can't see why hunting inside ephemeral would make sense).

We don't usually want to check gates except at "admission", or else we allow data in and then fail updates.  That said, I would drop a comment here with the gate name so that when we eventually remove the gate, we can grep for it and find this and remove the nil check.

PrepareForCreate() is called from BeforeCreate() is called from staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/registry/generic/registry/store.go Store.create().

createValidation (which includes admission plugins) is called AFTER callingBeforeCreate().

+
+package disableservicelinks
+
+import (
+	"context"
+	"fmt"
+	"io"
+	"k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/errors"
+	"k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/admission"
+	"k8s.io/kubernetes/pkg/apis/core"
+	"k8s.io/utils/ptr"
+)
+
+// PluginName indicates name of admission plugin.
+const PluginName = "DisableServiceLinks"
+
+// Register is called by the apiserver to register the plugin factory.
+func Register(plugins *admission.Plugins) {
+	plugins.Register(PluginName, func(config io.Reader) (admission.Interface, error) {
+		return newDisableServiceLinks(), nil
+	})
+}
+
+// newDisableServiceLinks creates a new instance of the DisableServiceLinks admission controller.
+func newDisableServiceLinks() *plugin {
+	return &plugin{
+		Handler: admission.NewHandler(admission.Create),
+	}
+}
+
+// Make sure we are implementing the interface.
+var _ admission.MutationInterface = &plugin{}
+
+type plugin struct {
+	*admission.Handler
+}
+
+// Admit updates the EnableServiceLinks of a pod and set it to false.
+func (p *plugin) Admit(ctx context.Context, attributes admission.Attributes, o admission.ObjectInterfaces) error {
+	// Ignore all calls to subresources or resources other than pods.
+	if len(attributes.GetSubresource()) != 0 || attributes.GetResource().GroupResource() != core.Resource("pods") {
+		return nil
+	}
+
+	pod, ok := attributes.GetObject().(*core.Pod)
+	if !ok {
+		return errors.NewBadRequest(fmt.Sprintf("expected *core.Pod but got %T", attributes.GetObject()))
+	}
+
+	pod.Spec.EnableServiceLinks = ptr.To(false)

Explain why seemingly-useless manual conversions are needed.  This is

Remove manual admissionregistration.Rule conversions

These might have been needed in the past, but no longer.  The ones that are left are a bad situation.

When we do codegen we: a) remove generated files; b) set a build tag to ignore generated files.  Then conversion-gen tool searches for specially-named conversion functions.  When there is a cross-API dependency, it can't find the functions (because (a) and (b) above) so they generate a compile error.  We should do better.  For now, docs++.

comment_text:
This is apparently a trick used by some Go devs to force fields to be named in struct initializing code (https://groups.google.com/g/Golang-Nuts/c/NSjVW82i0mY/m/qSshj0qIYxQJ).

From: https://github.com/kubernetes/gengo/issues/133

Running deepcopy-gen against structs that have `_` fields in them generates invalid go code.

Source struct:

```
type CreateBucketConfiguration struct {
	_ struct{} `type:"structure"`

	LocationConstraint BucketLocationConstraint `type:"string" enum:"true"`
}
type BucketLocationConstraint string
```

`doc.go` directives to deepcopy:

```
// +k8s:deepcopy-gen=package,register
```

deepcopy directives above structs that the above struct is a child of  (not sure if this makes a difference or not - this is autogenerated code from the operator sdk)

```
// +k8s:deepcopy-gen:interfaces=k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime.Object
```

Output by deepcopy-gen:
```
// DeepCopyInto is an autogenerated deepcopy function, copying the receiver, writing into out. in must be non-nil.
func (in *CreateBucketConfiguration) DeepCopyInto(out *CreateBucketConfiguration) {
	*out = *in
	out._ = in._
	return
}
```

Setting or accessing `_` fields is invalid. 

I applied the following patch locally and it outputs correct code (ie does not attempt to copy the `_` field).  No idea if this is the _correct_ way to solve the problem though.

```
diff --git a/vendor/k8s.io/gengo/examples/deepcopy-gen/generators/deepcopy.go b/vendor/k8s.io/gengo/examples/deepcopy-gen/generators/deepcopy.go
index 4548108..1d90578 100644
--- a/vendor/k8s.io/gengo/examples/deepcopy-gen/generators/deepcopy.go
+++ b/vendor/k8s.io/gengo/examples/deepcopy-gen/generators/deepcopy.go
@@ -823,6 +823,9 @@ func (g *genDeepCopy) doStruct(t *types.Type, sw *generator.SnippetWriter) {

 	// Now fix-up fields as needed.
 	for _, m := range ut.Members {
+		if m.Name == "_" {
+			continue
+		}
 		ft := m.Type
 		uft := underlyingType(ft)
```

After applying this patch, output is as expected:

```
// DeepCopyInto is an autogenerated deepcopy function, copying the receiver, writing into out. in must be non-nil.
func (in *CreateBucketConfiguration) DeepCopyInto(out *CreateBucketConfiguration) {
	*out = *in
	return
}
```

comment_text:
Adding this comment before reading all the rest - do we really need a builder?  The API (fields) has notc hanged in forever, so it seems pretty safe to make them public.  It's a little more verbose, but more "regular Go"

```
g := feature.Gate{
	Name: "foobar",
	Default: true,
	Stability: feature.Beta,
}
```

vs:

```
g := featuregates.NewFeatureGate("foobar").
	EnableByDefault().
	Beta().
	ToFeatureGate()
```

It's the same LOC (excpet a curly) and less likely to forget the last `ToFeatureGate()` (Go's lack of destructors makes this pattern dangerous, IMO.

+// ResourceIndexer wraps an indexer and resource for a given type.
+// This is intended for use by listers (generated by lister-gen) only.

+// This is intended for use by listers (generated by lister-gen) only.
+func New[T runtime.Object](indexer cache.Indexer, resource schema.GroupResource) ResourceIndexer[T] {
+	return ResourceIndexer[T]{indexer: indexer, resource: resource}
+}
+
+// NewNamespaced returns a new instance of a namespaced lister (resource indexer) wrapping the given parent and namespace for the specified type.
+// This is intended for use by listers (generated by lister-gen) only.

+	return NamespacedResourceIndexer[T]{parent, namespace}

+
+// List lists all resources in the indexer matching the given selector.
+func (l ResourceIndexer[T]) List(selector labels.Selector) (ret []T, err error) {
+	err = cache.ListAll(l.indexer, selector, func(m interface{}) {
+		ret = append(ret, m.(T))
+	})
+	return ret, err
+}
+
+// Get retrieves the resource from the index for a given name.
+func (l ResourceIndexer[T]) Get(name string) (T, error) {
+	obj, exists, err := l.indexer.GetByKey(name)
+	if err != nil {
+		return *new(T), err
+	}
+	if !exists {
+		return *new(T), errors.NewNotFound(l.resource, name)
+	}
+	return obj.(T), nil
+}
+

+	err = cache.ListAllByNamespace(l.indexer, l.namespace, selector, func(m interface{}) {
+		ret = append(ret, m.(T))
+	})
+	return ret, err
+}

Not sure I follow.  Let's pick on Service as an example.

if I add a `FooBar` method to `ServiceListerExpansion`, then `NewServiceLister()` (which returns a generated wrapper of `listers.ResourceIndexer[*v1.Service]`) fails to compile because  `ResourceIndexer[*v1.Service]` does not have a `FooBar` method and no longer satisfies `ServiceLister`.

How would someone use the extensions?  I'm not seeing it.

comment_text: I think the goal of this pattern for tests is to make every testcase spell out as LITTLE as possible.  Calling validation on `podtest.MakePod("foo")` should succeed.  Thenceforth, everything you specify in a testcase is germane to that specific testcase.  All these calls to `podtest.SetContainers(podtest.MakeContainer("ctr"))` can go away - they are just noise and are not related to the testcases (except when they are).

comment_text: Does this properly handle the case of a Pod's UID appearig to change?  For example, we miss an event (or are down while it happens) which deletes the Pod and recreates it with the same name, but slightly different config?  This has happened in ther places where the object was supposed to be immutable but people find ways around it, on purpose or otherwise.

That is what I am getting at.  Test cases that want an empty list will be have to explicitly set it to nil, which actually INCREASES their obviousness.  Test cases that focus on aspects of containers have to explicitly say so, and test cases that don't focus on containers say nothing.

Why is this making a new sysctl?  The whole point of having a sysctl interface is so that it can be manufactured one time and used repeatedly, and faked during tests.  This is a trivial function, but think how you would write a unit test for this which  covers both true and false paths?  

The downside of that is that it requires plumbing.

This function is called from `ensureHostsFile` <- `makeHostsMount` <- `makeMounts` <- `kubelet.GenerateRunContainerOptions` -- shouldn't kubelet have a `utilsysctl.Interface` that you could just pass down and then call `GetSystctl()` on?

Reading between the lines, it sounds like @tessapham may have GOFLAGS set (which is OK and we do want to respect), including -mod=mod (which is not OK).  That's the ony way I can reproduce the original report.  

We can either a) fail and let users change their calling env (most correct, IMO); b) unilaterally override GOFLAGS and set `-mod=vendor` (meaning you can't set -mod=readonly, which may be OK).

This seems to work (but would need comments, etc)

```diff
diff --git a/hack/lib/golang.sh b/hack/lib/golang.sh
index 1a65f82154b..dfc701951f4 100755
--- a/hack/lib/golang.sh
+++ b/hack/lib/golang.sh
@@ -589,6 +589,8 @@ kube::golang::setup_env() {
   unset GO111MODULE
   unset GOWORK
 
+  export GOFLAGS="${GOFLAGS:-} -mod=vendor"
+
   # This may try to download our specific Go version.  Do it last so it uses
   # the above-configured environment.
   kube::golang::internal::verify_go_version
```

Perhaps we should just do:

```diff
commit 63660ab0f20330af5adad795d5325f6c8b2ea984
Good "git" signature for thockin@google.com with ED25519 key SHA256:PfQ0rwNUgsu5aRmerT0vkihWn/S3MXY3uoCPUiMdPrg
Author: Tim Hockin <thockin@google.com>
Date:   Wed May 22 09:22:35 2024 -0700

    hack

diff --git a/hack/lib/golang.sh b/hack/lib/golang.sh
index dfc701951f4..1f44d579413 100755
--- a/hack/lib/golang.sh
+++ b/hack/lib/golang.sh
@@ -558,6 +558,12 @@ EOF
 #   env-var GOBIN is unset (we want binaries in a predictable place)
 #   env-var PATH includes the local GOPATH
 kube::golang::setup_env() {
+  # Sometimes users want to override Go build flags, but it's rare and can
+  # cause build failures (e.g. setting '-mod=mod' will not work).
+  if [[ -n "${GOFLAGS}" ]]; then
+      kube::log::info "WARNING: GOFLAGS is set, which may cause build issues: ${GOFLAGS}"
+  fi
+
   # Even in module mode, we need to set GOPATH for `go build` and `go install`
   # to work.  We build various tools (usually via `go install`) from a lot of
   # scripts.
```
